translation: The command of Allah is coming, so be not impatient for it. Exalted is He and high above what they associate with Him
commentary:
أَتَىٰٓ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

This surah begins with an urgency that marks its placement in the later Makkan period. The opening declaration is striking: "The decision of Allah ﷻ has already come." Notice the past tense—أَتَىٰ. For Allah ﷻ, who owns all of time, the future is already accomplished. The punishment decreed for the disbelievers is as certain as if it had already occurred.

Yet immediately following this past-tense declaration comes a command in the present: فَلَا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ —"so do not rush it." The verb is in the doubled form, emphasizing the prohibition. This linguistic shift is profound. When Allah ﷻ speaks of His own decree, He uses past tense because from His perspective, it's already done. When He addresses humanity, He switches to present tense because we exist within the flow of time, waiting for what He has already determined.

The ayah concludes with سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ —declaring His absolute perfection and elevation above whatever they associate with Him. This is more than a statement of monotheism. It's a reminder that the One who controls time itself, who speaks of the future in past tense, is incomparably above the false deities they worship.

translation: He sends down the angels, with the inspiration of His command, upon whom He wills of His servants, [telling them], "Warn that there is no deity except Me; so fear Me
commentary:
يُنَزِّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ بِٱلرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦٓ أَنْ أَنذِرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱتَّقُونِ

Allah ﷻ sends down angels with الرُّوحِ —the spirit, the revelation. This word carries multiple layers of meaning. Revelation itself is called ruh because it gives life to dead hearts. Angel Jibreel (AS) himself is referred to as Ar-Ruh. The revelation comes مِنْ أَمْرِهِۦ —from His command—upon whomever He wishes from among His slaves.

The message these angels carry is profound in its simplicity: أَنذِرُوٓا۟ —"Warn!" This command addresses all messengers across all generations as though they stand together receiving one unified instruction. The warning itself is the foundation of all faith: لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠—there is no deity worthy of worship except Me.

The ayah ends with فَٱتَّقُونِ —"so be mindful of Me, protect yourselves from My wrath." This isn't just about belief; it's about living with consciousness of the One who sent the warning.

translation: He created the heavens and earth in truth. High is He above what they associate with Him
commentary:
خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ تَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

He created the heavens and the earth بِٱلْحَقِّ —with truth, with purpose, with justice. This isn't a universe born from randomness or play. Everything has meaning, everything has design. The phrase تَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ repeats from the first ayah, reinforcing that the Creator of all reality is far above the partners they ascribe to Him.

translation: He created man from a sperm-drop; then at once, he is a clear adversary
commentary:
خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ فَإِذَا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُّبِينٌ

Now Allah ﷻ turns His attention to the human being with a humbling reminder. He created the human from نُّطْفَةٍ —a drop of fluid. The word nutfah literally refers to something so insignificant you'd want to wash it off your clothes if it got on them. It's deliberately undignified.

Yet from this humble beginning, فَإِذَا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُّبِينٌ —suddenly he becomes an open arguer, a clear disputer. The progression is almost comical in its irony. Yesterday you were a drop of worthless fluid, and today you think you can argue against your Creator? This ayah places the human being in proper perspective: Who do you think you are?

translation: And the grazing livestock He has created for you; in them is warmth and [numerous] benefits, and from them you eat
commentary:
وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمَ خَلَقَهَا ۗ لَكُمْ فِيهَا دِفْءٌ وَمَنَـٰفِعُ وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ

The mention of الْأَنْعَـٰمَ —the cattle—is introduced with a grammatical structure called majrur, where the object comes before the verb. This construction indicates special emphasis, as though Allah ﷻ is saying, "Pay attention to this creation I'm about to mention."

In these animals, there is دِفْءٌ —warmth. This refers to their hides and skins that provide clothing and shelter. There are مَنَـٰفِعُ —various benefits including milk and leather. And from them, you eat. The cattle are a complete provision system, meeting multiple human needs simultaneously.

translation: And for you in them is [the enjoyment of] beauty when you bring them in [for the evening] and when you send them out [to pasture]
commentary:
وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا جَمَالٌ حِينَ تُرِيحُونَ وَحِينَ تَسْرَحُونَ

But then Allah ﷻ mentions something unexpected: جَمَالٌ —beauty. There is beauty for you in the cattle. When is this beauty most apparent? حِينَ تُرِيحُونَ—in the late afternoon when you're bringing them back from grazing, as the sun begins to set. The scene of cattle slowly walking back to the farm after a day's work, silhouetted against the evening sky, creates a peaceful beauty that the farmer recognizes even after his labor.

وَحِينَ تَسْرَحُونَ—and in the morning when you take them out for grazing. The sunrise illuminating the animals as they head out to pasture creates another moment of aesthetic appreciation. Allah ﷻ didn't just give us utility in His creation; He gave us beauty to appreciate. To truly taste these ayahs, you have to experience them. Visit a farm. Observe the animals. Watch the sunrise and sunset with them. The Quran comes alive in these experiences.

translation: And they carry your loads to a land you could not have reached except with difficulty to yourselves. Indeed, your Lord is Kind and Merciful
commentary:
وَتَحْمِلُ أَثْقَالَكُمْ إِلَىٰ بَلَدٍ لَّمْ تَكُونُوا۟ بَـٰلِغِيهِ إِلَّا بِشِقِّ ٱلْأَنفُسِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ

These cattle carry أَثْقَالَكُمْ —your heavy loads—to distant lands that you would never reach إِلَّا بِشِقِّ ٱلْأَنفُسِ —except by breaking yourselves open, by cracking under the strain. Imagine traveling with all your luggage on your back. You'd be destroyed by the journey.

In the time of revelation, this referred to camels. In our time, it extends to cars, planes, ships—all the means of transport that carry our burdens. This is the ayah to recite when you're packing for a road trip, when you see how much baggage you're carrying and realize you could never manage it on your own strength.

إِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ—Certainly your Master is truly compassionate, always merciful. The word رَءُوفٌ carries a nuance of understanding what someone is going through. It's not just abstract mercy; it's the compassion of One who knows your struggle intimately.

translation: And [He created] the horses, mules and donkeys for you to ride and [as] adornment. And He creates that which you do not know
commentary:
وَٱلْخَيْلَ وَٱلْبِغَالَ وَٱلْحَمِيرَ لِتَرْكَبُوهَا وَزِينَةً ۚ وَيَخْلُقُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

Allah ﷻ begins with الْخَيْلَ —horses, the most noble of riding animals—then mentions mules and donkeys. All of these serve لِتَرْكَبُوهَا —for you to ride them—وَزِينَةً —and as a display of beauty. Even in practical transportation, there's an aesthetic element.

Then comes a fascinating addition: وَيَخْلُقُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ—"And He creates what you do not know." This opens the door to all future discoveries. Modern marine biologists believe the majority of species in the ocean remain unknown to us. This phrase acknowledges that Allah's ﷻ creation extends far beyond what we've catalogued or can even imagine.

translation: And upon Allah is the direction of the [right] way, and among the various paths are those deviating. And if He willed, He could have guided you all
commentary:
وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ قَصْدُ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَمِنْهَا جَآئِرٌ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ لَهَدَىٰكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

The word قَصْدُ comes from a root meaning straight, direct. When used as an intensive adjective, it means the straightest, the most direct. The straightest of all paths leads to Allah وَمِنْهَا جَآئِرٌ  .ﷻ —and from these paths, there are those that veer off, that deviate.

وَلَوْ شَآءَ لَهَدَىٰكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ—Had He willed, He would have guided all of you together. This acknowledges human free will within divine sovereignty. The straight path is available, clearly marked. Whether we choose to walk it or deviate is our test.

translation: It is He who sends down rain from the sky; from it is drink and from it is foliage in which you pasture [animals]
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً ۖ لَّكُم مِّنْهُ شَرَابٌ وَمِنْهُ شَجَرٌ فِيهِ تُسِيمُونَ

He is the One who sent down water from the sky. From it, لَّكُم مِّنْهُ شَرَابٌ—there is drink for you. وَمِنْهُ شَجَرٌ—and from it come trees. فِيهِ تُسِيمُونَ—in which you graze your animals, where they find shade and sustenance. The imagery continues to build on pastoral life, on shepherding, on the relationship between humans, animals and the natural world.

translation: He causes to grow for you thereby the crops, olives, palm trees, grapevines, and from all the fruits. Indeed in that is a sign for a people who give thought
commentary:
يُنۢبِتُ لَكُم بِهِ ٱلزَّرْعَ وَٱلزَّيْتُونَ وَٱلنَّخِيلَ وَٱلْأَعْنَـٰبَ وَمِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ

By means of this water, He causes crops to grow for you—olives, date palms, grapes, and every kind of fruit. The specificity of these fruits isn't random. They represent staple foods of the region, each with unique benefits and characteristics.

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ—In all of this, there is truly a miraculous sign for people who engage in deep thought. The standard here is high: yatafakkarun, from tafakkur, meaning profound contemplation. These ayahs should be experienced while sitting on a farm, watching crops grow, tasting their fruits. The reflection deepens when connected to direct experience.

translation: And He has subjected for you the night and day and the sun and moon, and the stars are subjected by His command. Indeed in that are signs for a people who reason
commentary:
وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ ۖ وَٱلنُّجُومُ مُسَخَّرَٰتٌۢ بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ

He has subjected for you the night and the day, the sun and the moon. The stars too are subjected by His command. These celestial bodies don't operate randomly—they move مُسَخَّرَٰتٌۢ بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ, subdued and controlled by His command.

Notice the shift: إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ—in this there are signs for people who at least use their intellect. The standard has been lowered from deep contemplation (tafakkur) to basic reasoning ('aql). You don't need access to farmland to observe the night, day, sun, moon and stars. At minimum, think about these.

translation: And [He has subjected] whatever He multiplied for you on the earth of varying colors. Indeed in that is a sign for a people who remember
commentary:
وَمَا ذَرَأَ لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُخْتَلِفًا أَلْوَٰنُهُۥٓ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ

And whatever He has created and distributed for you in the earth, مُخْتَلِفًا أَلْوَٰنُهُۥٓ—different in colors and textures. The grammatical construction here places the description before the described (mukhtalifan alwanuhu), a form of eloquence that emphasizes the diversity.

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ—in this is a sign for people who remember, who want to make an effort to remember. The standard drops even further. To remember (tadhakkur) requires less effort than to understand ('aql), which requires less than deep contemplation (tafakkur). The progression shows Allah ﷻ meeting people where they are, at whatever level of reflection they can manage.

translation: And it is He who subjected the sea for you to eat from it tender meat and to extract from it ornaments which you wear. And you see the ships plowing through it, and [He subjected it] that you may seek of His bounty; and perhaps you will be grateful
commentary:
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى سَخَّرَ ٱلْبَحْرَ لِتَأْكُلُوا۟ مِنْهُ لَحْمًا طَرِيًّا وَتَسْتَخْرِجُوا۟ مِنْهُ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَهَا وَتَرَى ٱلْفُلْكَ مَوَاخِرَ فِيهِ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ

He is the One who subjected the sea لِتَأْكُلُوا۟ مِنْهُ لَحْمًا طَرِيًّا—so you can eat from it fresh meat, referring to seafood. This is the ayah to recite when eating seafood, connecting your meal to the One who provided it.

وَتَسْتَخْرِجُوا۟ مِنْهُ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَهَا—and you extract from it jewelry that you wear. Pearls, coral—ornaments from the sea. This is the ayah to remember when buying or wearing jewelry.

وَتَرَى ٱلْفُلْكَ مَوَاخِرَ فِيهِ—and you see the ships piercing through it. The word مَوَاخِرَ paints a vivid picture: the ships rip through the water, leaving a wake behind them. When you're on a boat and see that trail of disturbed water stretching behind you, that's mawakhira—the visual evidence of the ship's passage.

وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ—and so you can seek from His bounty through trade and commerce. وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ—and so that all of you may become grateful. Every benefit mentioned leads to this conclusion: gratitude.

translation: And He has cast into the earth firmly set mountains, lest it shift with you, and [made] rivers and roads, that you may be guided
commentary:
وَأَلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ وَأَنْهَـٰرًا وَسُبُلًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ

Allah ﷻ brings us back from the ocean to the land. He threw down into the earth رَوَٰسِىَ —firm, stable mountains that penetrate deep. أَن تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ—so that you wouldn't slip, so the earth wouldn't shift beneath you. Mountains stabilize the ground we walk on.

وَأَنْهَـٰرًا—and rivers. This is an eloquent construction where the verb is implied. He didn't throw rivers down; rather, when the mountains came, they gave birth to rivers through waterfalls and underground springs. وَسُبُلًا—and paths, valleys, roads formed by and between these geographical features. لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ—so you may be guided. This guidance operates on multiple levels: guided to Islam, but also guided along the physical paths to reach your destination, your village, your city.

translation: And landmarks. And by the stars they are [also] guided
commentary:
وَعَلَـٰمَـٰتٍ ۚ وَبِٱلنَّجْمِ هُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ

وَعَلَـٰمَـٰتٍ—and landmarks. These mountains, rivers and distinctive rocks serve as markers for navigation. وَبِٱلنَّجْمِ هُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ—and by the stars they find guidance and direction. Before GPS, travelers navigated by celestial and terrestrial landmarks. To truly experience these ayahs, you need to go out into nature, away from the city lights, and see how the natural world provides its own guidance system.

translation: Then is He who creates like one who does not create? So will you not be reminded
commentary:
أَفَمَن يَخْلُقُ كَمَن لَّا يَخْلُقُ ۗ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ

After this extensive catalogue of creation, Allah ﷻ poses a rhetorical question: Is the One who creates like the one who doesn't create? The comparison is absurd on its face. Your false gods create nothing—they themselves are created. How can they be compared to the Creator of everything you've just been reminded of?

أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ—Why don't you make an effort to remember? Won't you reflect on this obvious truth? The question challenges the listener to connect the dots, to see the impossibility of ascribing partners to the One who alone creates, sustains and controls all that exists.

translation: And if you should count the favors of Allah, you could not enumerate them. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَإِن تَعُدُّوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ لَا تُحْصُوهَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

If you were to try counting the favors of Allah ﷻ, you would never be able to encompass them all. This ayah builds on themes we've already encountered in the previous surah. The phrase "you would never be able to encompass them" carries a deeper meaning than simple counting. The word أَحْصَى implies not just enumeration but complete comprehension and full appreciation.

What makes this ayah particularly striking is its conclusion: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - "Indeed, Allah ﷻ is Forgiving and Merciful." At first glance, this might seem disconnected from the discussion of blessings. But there's a powerful contrast at work here. The previous surah established a tension: you are so messed up, yet I am so forgiving. This isn't just about listing Allah's gifts; it's about recognizing our complete inadequacy in the face of His overwhelming generosity. We can't even count His blessings, let alone thank Him properly for them, yet He remains Forgiving and Merciful despite our failures.

translation: And Allah knows what you conceal and what you declare
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُسِرُّونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ

Allah ﷻ knows what you keep secret and what you make public. This knowledge encompasses every dimension of human existence. All of us have aspects of our personality that remain hidden from others. We all maintain private thoughts, private actions, private dimensions of who we are that we would never want anyone else to discover.

This isn't about hypocrisy in the negative sense. It's simply natural human behavior. We don't let everything hang out in public. We reserve ourselves. We watch the way we speak, the way we carry ourselves, how we sit, how we talk, how we act. At least someone was raised with some manners. We naturally present the best versions of ourselves to others.

But Allah ﷻ knows both dimensions completely. He knows your private life and your public life, what you're keeping secret and what you're announcing. In the context of the disbelievers of Makkah, this takes on additional meaning: He knows the criticisms you're making on the outside. He knows that you've actually come to accept this message on the inside, but you just don't want to make the changes in your life that acceptance would require.

translation: And those they invoke other than Allah create nothing, and they [themselves] are created
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ لَا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ

Those they call upon besides Allah ﷻ don't create anything. They themselves are created. The gods the Quraysh worship, the rocks, the trees, whatever entities they've elevated to divine status, possess no creative power whatsoever. The contrast is absolute: these objects you worship cannot bring anything into existence, yet they themselves were brought into existence by something else.

translation: They are, [in fact], dead, not alive, and they do not perceive when they will be resurrected
commentary:
أَمْوَٰتٌ غَيْرُ أَحْيَآءٍ ۖ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ

They are dead, without any life in them. They have no sensation whatsoever. The word أَيَّانَ means "when," and يُبْعَثُونَ means "they will be raised." These lifeless objects you worship don't even possess awareness. They have no recognition of when they will be raised on the Day of Resurrection. They themselves don't know their own fate.

translation: Your god is one God. But those who do not believe in the Hereafter - their hearts are disapproving, and they are arrogant
commentary:
إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۚ فَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ قُلُوبُهُم مُّنكِرَةٌ وَهُم مُّسْتَكْبِرُونَ

Your God is One God. This declaration of Tawheed appears repeatedly throughout the Quran, and one of its profound benefits becomes clear when you consider the audience. You're speaking to a community, to an entire city, that believes in multiple gods. What does polytheism create psychologically? It creates escape routes. It creates scapegoats.

If you're in trouble with one god, you can run to another god for help. Think about how children operate between parents. When you're in trouble with one parent, where do you go? The other parent. And the really strategic move is to say, "But Mom told me to do that," pitting the parents against each other. In Greek mythology, one of the most popular mythologies about multiple gods, the gods themselves are constantly fighting with one another, competing for the loyalties of human beings.

The religion of Tawheed essentially removes all of these escape routes. Allah ﷻ is telling humanity: You don't go to anybody else. You come to Me. You don't deal with anybody else. You deal with Me. You cannot ignore Me. You cannot deny Me. This isn't merely theological; it's deeply psychological.

As for those who don't believe in the Afterlife, their hearts are in a state of denial. And once the heart enters denial, what's the only thing left? مُّسْتَكْبِرُونَ - they become people filled with arrogance. Notice the connection: the contrast isn't between one who believes in multiple gods versus one who believes in One God. The contrast is between the one who believes in One God and the one who is arrogant.

When you truly realize that Allah ﷻ is One and you think about Him constantly, you begin to see yourself as nothing. Your pride evaporates. You recognize your own insignificance before His majesty.

translation: Assuredly, Allah knows what they conceal and what they declare. Indeed, He does not like the arrogant
commentary:
لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُسْتَكْبِرِينَ

لَا جَرَمَ is roughly translated as "no doubt about it," but it carries additional weight. Some scholars believe it implies an oath: "I swear by Allah ﷻ." The phrase always appears with أَنَّ, never without it. Because أَنَّ already implies emphasis, and لَا جَرَمَ adds to that emphasis, you have a doubled form of certainty. It's emphatic upon emphatic.

No doubt about it whatsoever: Allah ﷻ knows what they keep secret and what they expose. He does not love those who become arrogant, who seek their own greatness, who elevate themselves above the truth.

translation: And when it is said to them, "What has your Lord sent down?" They say, "Legends of the former peoples
commentary:
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُم مَّاذَآ أَنزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ ۙ قَالُوٓا۟ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

When it's said to them, "What did your Master send down?" they say, "Legends of old times." The word أَسَاطِير is plural; the singular is أُسْطُورَة. This word came into Arabic from Greek—related to the English word "story"—and carries the connotation of myths, legends, folklore.

This dismissive attitude isn't new to our age. If you study Islam today at elite universities with Islamic studies programs, what is their fundamental thesis by the end of it all? The Quran is just an interesting narrative. That's what it boils down to: just a collection of stories. The story of the Prophet ﷺ? That's just a story to them. The accounts of nations that came before? Myths and folk tales. It's studied as folk culture, as something beneath the modern mind, something the modern mind examines as entertainment rather than as something above and beyond human capability.

This attitude of belittling the Quran, of reducing it to mere stories, has existed from the very beginning. The Quraysh said the same thing: "These are just legends of old times. What am I supposed to do with ancient stories?"

translation: That they may bear their own burdens in full on the Day of Resurrection and some of the burdens of those whom they misguide without knowledge. Unquestionably, evil is that which they bear
commentary:
لِيَحْمِلُوٓا۟ أَوْزَارَهُمْ كَامِلَةً يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۙ وَمِنْ أَوْزَارِ ٱلَّذِينَ يُضِلُّونَهُم بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۗ أَلَا سَآءَ مَا يَزِرُونَ

They will carry their heavy burdens—أَوْزَار, their loads—all of them, completely, on the Day of Resurrection. But it doesn't end there. They will also carry part of the burdens of those whom they misguided without knowledge.

This phrase carries two possible meanings. First, they didn't realize they were misguiding others—they were reckless with their influence. Second, and perhaps more likely, those who were misguided had no knowledge, which is why they were so easily led astray. The misleaders will bear not only their own sins but also the weight of having caused others to sin.

أَلَا سَآءَ مَا يَزِرُونَ - "What a horrible load they are carrying!" Notice the connection. Earlier in this surah, you were told about carrying luggage. If you try to carry your luggage by yourself, what happens? Your back will break, crack in half. Now these people will carry all of their luggage of evil deeds, all of it at once, along with the burden of everyone they misled.

translation: Those before them had already plotted, but Allah came at their building from the foundations, so the roof fell upon them from above them, and the punishment came to them from where they did not perceive
commentary:
قَدْ مَكَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَأَتَى ٱللَّهُ بُنْيَـٰنَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْقَوَاعِدِ فَخَرَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلسَّقْفُ مِن فَوْقِهِمْ وَأَتَىٰهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ

Those who came before them plotted and schemed as well. Then Allah ﷻ attacked their buildings from the very foundations—مِنَ ٱلْقَوَاعِدِ, from the base structures themselves.

What's the strongest part of any building? The foundation. When a hailstorm comes, when wind attacks, it damages everything else, but what remains standing at the end? The foundation. That's what survives destruction. But Allah ﷻ says: When I attack, I start with the foundation itself.

فَخَرَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلسَّقْفُ - the roof, the ceiling, caves in on them, falls upon them from above. In modern demolition, how do they bring down massive buildings? They place explosives in the foundation, and the entire structure collapses in on itself. If you want to see a visual example of what this ayah describes, watch a controlled demolition—the foundation is destroyed, and the roof falls on top of everything below.

وَأَتَىٰهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ - and the punishment came at them from where they had not even realized, from a direction they never anticipated.

translation: Then on the Day of Resurrection He will disgrace them and say, "Where are My 'partners' for whom you used to oppose [the believers]?" Those who were given knowledge will say, "Indeed disgrace, this Day, and evil are upon the disbelievers
commentary:
ثُمَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يُخْزِيهِمْ وَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تُشَـٰٓقُّونَ فِيهِمْ ۚ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ إِنَّ ٱلْخِزْىَ ٱلْيَوْمَ وَٱلسُّوٓءَ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

Then, on the Day of Resurrection, He will humiliate them. Allah ﷻ will say, "Where are these associates of Mine? Where is Zeus? Where is Hercules? Where is Jesus (AS)? Where is Mary? Where is the Holy Ghost? What happened to them? I don't see them here."

أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تُشَـٰٓقُّونَ فِيهِمْ - "Where are these partners of Mine that you used to be so defiant about?" The word تُشَـٰٓقُّونَ means to argue, to debate, to oppose, to show vicious opposition. You were so offended if anything was said against your gods. You were ready to fight for them, ready to defend their honor. It doesn't seem like they're ready to stand for you now. Where did they go?

Those who have been given knowledge—the believers, the Muslims—will speak on that day, saying: "Indeed, humiliation today and the worst kind of outcome have come upon the disbelievers."

translation: The ones whom the angels take in death [while] wronging themselves, and [who] then offer submission, [saying], "We were not doing any evil." But, yes! Indeed, Allah is Knowing of what you used to do
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ظَالِمِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ فَأَلْقَوُا۟ ٱلسَّلَمَ مَا كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ مِن سُوٓءٍۭ ۚ بَلَىٰٓ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

Those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves. Picture this: someone is living their life, indulging, partying, immersed in sin. In the middle of all that, Allah ﷻ takes them. The angel comes and extracts their soul, and suddenly they're no longer in this world. They're in the realm of the Barzakh, standing before consequences they never prepared for.

فَأَلْقَوُا۟ ٱلسَّلَمَ - they throw down their submission before the angels. It's a gesture of panic and desperation. مَا كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ مِن سُوٓءٍۭ - "We weren't doing anything wrong! You've got it all mixed up! I was just here with my friends. This is a misunderstanding!"

This behavior isn't limited to disbelievers. Think about when a Muslim kid gets caught doing something wrong. What's the first response? "No, no, no, no! That wasn't me! He made me do it!" It's exactly the same behavior, the same instinct to deflect and deny.

The angels respond: بَلَىٰٓ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ - "No, no. Allah ﷻ knew what you were up to, without any doubt. He knew all along. We don't need your explanations."

translation: So enter the gates of Hell to abide eternally therein, and how wretched is the residence of the arrogant
commentary:
فَٱدْخُلُوٓا۟ أَبْوَٰبَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ فَلَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ

Enter the gates of Jahannam. You will remain in it permanently. فَلَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ - what a horrible, long-term place of stay for those who were full of themselves, for those consumed by arrogance.

translation: And it will be said to those who feared Allah, "What did your Lord send down?" They will say, "[That which is] good." For those who do good in this world is good; and the home of the Hereafter is better. And how excellent is the home of the righteous
commentary:
۞ وَقِيلَ لِلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ مَاذَآ أَنزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ ۚ قَالُوا۟ خَيْرًا ۗ لِّلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةٌ ۚ وَلَدَارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ ۚ وَلَنِعْمَ دَارُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ

Now the scene shifts dramatically. It was said to those who protected themselves, who lived with taqwa, who worked not to disobey Allah ﷻ or displease Him: "What did your Master send down?"

When the disbelievers were asked this question, they responded with mockery: "Legends of old times." But when the believers are asked, their response is immediate and genuine: خَيْرًا - "It is good."

That's it. One word. But what a word. Their tone, their emphasis conveys everything. It's so amazing. It's so beautiful. It's so much better than anything else. This response has deep meaning. The way they answer reveals that they have tasted revelation. They have a relationship with it.

Think about how you speak when you've genuinely experienced something meaningful. When someone asks you about an incredible game, you don't give a flat response. You say, "It was amazing! Double overtime!" When someone asks about a movie that moved you: "The most amazing thing I've ever seen." When someone asks about a beautiful place: "The weather was perfect. The ocean was unbelievable."

You speak with that kind of passion because you experienced it. You lived it. When believers are asked about revelation, it's not a Sunday school answer: "The Quran is good because God sent it." That's not what's expected here. You and I must taste revelation. We must experience it deeply so that we can say genuinely, from the core of our being: خَيْرًا.

لِّلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةٌ - those who excelled, who did ihsan in this world, will have the most beautiful thing. وَلَدَارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ - and the home of the Afterlife is in fact even better. وَلَنِعْمَ دَارُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ - and what an awesome home for those who protected themselves through taqwa.

translation: Gardens of perpetual residence, which they will enter, beneath which rivers flow. They will have therein whatever they wish. Thus does Allah reward the righteous
commentary:
جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَهَا تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ لَهُمْ فِيهَا مَا يَشَآءُونَ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَجْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ

Gardens of Eden—جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ —they will enter them. تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ - rivers flow beneath them, at their feet. Imagine: your foot touching the edge of a river. One river leads into another. They all begin with waterfalls. Wherever you want to swim, there's another body of water waiting.

لَهُمْ فِيهَا مَا يَشَآءُونَ - they will have in it whatever they want. Notice the word: مَا يَشَآءُونَ - "whatever they will" or "whatever they wish." They don't even have to ask. All they have to do is want something and it appears. You're sitting there thinking, "This place is so perfect, but a basketball court right about there would be beautiful," and it materializes.

كَذَٰلِكَ يَجْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ - this is how He compensates the people who lived with taqwa, who remained conscious of Him throughout their lives.

translation: The ones whom the angels take in death, [being] good and pure; [the angels] will say, "Peace be upon you. Enter Paradise for what you used to do
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ ۙ يَقُولُونَ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

Remember how the angels took away some people while they were wronging themselves? Now we see the opposite scenario. Those whom the angels take away طَيِّبِينَ - while they are good, while they are in a state of purity and goodness. This also encourages us to remain in a state of wudu, to be in a constant state of spiritual preparedness.

The angels say to them: سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمُ - "Peace be upon you." ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ - "Enter Jannah." بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ - "Because of what you used to do, because of the actions you performed in the world."

translation: Do the disbelievers await [anything] except that the angels should come to them or there comes the command of your Lord? Thus did those do before them. And Allah wronged them not, but they had been wronging themselves
commentary:
هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِىَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ فَعَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ وَمَا ظَلَمَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ

Are they going to keep waiting around, refusing to take any action, until the angels themselves come for them, or until the decision of your Master arrives? كَذَٰلِكَ فَعَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ - that is exactly what those who came long before them did as well. They delayed, they procrastinated, they waited until it was too late.

وَمَا ظَلَمَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ - Allah ﷻ did not wrong them at all. He gave them time, He sent them messengers, He provided them with signs and guidance. وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ - but they used to wrong themselves. They were their own oppressors.

translation: So they were struck by the evil consequences of what they did and were enveloped by what they used to ridicule
commentary:
فَأَصَابَهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ

Then the evils of what they had done came and struck them. Notice the phrasing: سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ - the harms, the consequences of whatever they had earned. It doesn't say "the punishment of Allah ﷻ attacked them." It says your own deeds attacked you. Your own sins came back and struck you down.

وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ - and the very thing they used to mock, the very thing they used to joke about and dismiss, came and encircled them, surrounded them completely, and took hold of them with no escape.

translation: And those who associate others with Allah say, "If Allah had willed, we would not have worshipped anything other than Him, neither we nor our fathers, nor would we have forbidden anything through other than Him." Thus did those do before them. So is there upon the messengers except [the duty of] clear notification
commentary:
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا عَبَدْنَا مِن دُونِهِۦ مِن شَىْءٍ نَّحْنُ وَلَآ ءَابَآؤُنَا وَلَا حَرَّمْنَا مِن دُونِهِۦ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ فَعَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ فَهَلْ عَلَى ٱلرُّسُلِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ

This ayah addresses one of the most important theological questions in human history. Those who committed shirk said, "If Allah ﷻ had willed, we would not have worshiped anything besides Him—نَّحْنُ وَلَآ ءَابَآؤُنَا - neither we nor our ancestors would have done it."

This is the argument from predestination: "It's not our fault. If God already decided that I'm going to be a mushrik, that's God's will. That's how it was meant to be. We wouldn't have done it if He hadn't wanted us to."

وَلَا حَرَّمْنَا مِن دُونِهِۦ مِن شَىْءٍ - "And we would not have forbidden anything or sanctified anything other than Him in any way, shape or form. This is all just how it's supposed to be."

I actually heard a preacher make this exact argument recently. He said, "The majority of people on this Earth are Christians. That shows you it's God's will. God wants us to be Christians. If Allah ﷻ didn't want it, we wouldn't be this way. Look at how many we are—it must be God's will."

كَذَٰلِكَ فَعَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ - that's exactly what those who came long before them also said. This argument isn't new. It's the oldest philosophical problem: the question of free will versus predestination, divine decree versus human responsibility.

How does Allah ﷻ respond to this problem? The response is phenomenal: فَهَلْ عَلَى ٱلرُّسُلِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ - "Is there any responsibility on the messenger except communicating the message clearly?"

The meaning is profound. Had you been listening to the Quran with a sincere heart, you would not have asked such a dishonest question. You would not have made such a dishonest claim. The job of the messenger is not to engage with you in philosophical debates about God's will and human agency. His job is simply to explain the Quran clearly.

If you have a clean conscience, this question won't even arise. It's not fundamentally a problem of philosophy; it's a problem of a dirty conscience. That's what this really is. The messenger is simply supposed to deliver the message with clarity. Don't be distracted by these intellectual smokescreens.

We're learning something critical about dawah here. Never allow yourself to be distracted from what we have to share with people. What we have to show people is straightforward. The message is clear. All messengers were told the same thing: keep it simple, keep it direct, and trust that sincere hearts will recognize the truth.

translation: And We certainly sent into every nation a messenger, [saying], "Worship Allah and avoid Taghut." And among them were those whom Allah guided, and among them were those upon whom error was [deservedly] decreed. So proceed through the earth and observe how was the end of the deniers
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ بَعَثْنَا فِى كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَّسُولًا أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتَ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةُ ۚ فَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ

This ayah begins with a powerful declaration: We have certainly raised in every nation a messenger. The word بَعَثْنَا (ba'athna) means we appointed or we raised, indicating that Allah ﷻ never left any community without guidance. Every nation received a messenger with the same fundamental message: أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ—that you all should worship Allah—وَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتَ—and that all of you should avoid and stay away from the forces of rebellion.

This ayah serves as Allah's ﷻ commentary on the previous conversation where the disbelievers claimed, "If Allah wanted, we wouldn't have committed shirk." Allah ﷻ responds by clarifying that the real problem isn't divine will—the real problem is behavioral. The issue is that people cannot seem to stay away from taghut, the forces that call to rebellion against Allah ﷻ. They cannot let go of their loyalties to those who oppose divine guidance.

The ayah continues: فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ—among them are those who Allah guided—وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةُ—and among them are those upon whom misguidance was materialized, became a reality. The phrase حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةُ is particularly significant. It means that misguidance was rightfully deserved for them, that it was right for them. In other words, they earned it through their choices and resistance to the truth.

Allah ﷻ then commands: فَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟—go around in the earth, travel the land, and then take a look for yourselves—كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ—what was the outcome of those who lied, who made claims against the truth and denied it? History itself becomes a witness, offering lessons from the ruins of nations that rejected their messengers.

translation: [Even] if you should strive for their guidance, [O Muhammad], indeed, Allah does not guide those He sends astray, and they will have no helpers
commentary:
إِن تَحْرِصْ عَلَىٰ هُدَىٰهُمْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى مَن يُضِلُّ ۖ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ

This ayah addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly, acknowledging the deep pain He felt in wanting to guide His people: إِن تَحْرِصْ عَلَىٰ هُدَىٰهُمْ—if you are zealous, if you are really enthusiastic and looking forward to guiding them—فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى مَن يُضِلُّ—then know that Allah does not guide whoever He decides to misguide.

This was particularly difficult for the Messenger ﷺ to accept. These weren't strangers—they were His own people, His tribe. He thought, "I'm the messenger to all of humanity, yet I can't even save my own people. So many of them are going to reject the message." But Allah ﷻ does not measure the success or failure of His messenger by how many of his own people accept guidance.

This reality resonates in our own lives. People in our own families can be the hardest to reach. Sometimes starting a conversation about faith with them feels impossible. We cannot force guidance upon anyone. As Allah ﷻ told His beloved Prophet ﷺ in another surah: إِنَّكَ لَا تَهْدِى مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ—you do not guide whoever you love. When Allah ﷻ has decided to misguide someone based on their choices and rejection, it's done for them.

This doesn't mean we give up on people. We continue to remind, we continue to call, but we do so keeping in mind that what we're doing is not going to guide them—it is Allah ﷻ alone who guides. The ayah concludes: وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ—and they're not going to have any helpers or aids whatsoever on the Day of Judgment.

translation: And they swear by Allah their strongest oaths [that] Allah will not resurrect one who dies. But yes - [it is] a true promise [binding] upon Him, but most of the people do not know
commentary:
وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ ۙ لَا يَبْعَثُ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَمُوتُ ۚ بَلَىٰ وَعْدًا عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

The disbelievers made Allah ﷻ a witness in their oaths: وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ—they swore to Allah having exerted the strongest forms of oath. The word جَهْدَ (jahd) means to put tremendous effort into something. They were really loud, really emotional when they took their oaths, declaring: لَا يَبْعَثُ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَمُوتُ—Allah is not going to raise anybody who dies.

Allah's ﷻ response is emphatic: بَلَىٰ—but on the contrary! وَعْدًا عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا—it is a promise that is mandated upon Him, truly. In other words, it's a demand of Allah's ﷻ justice that He will raise people and bring them to account. Such a promise is mandated upon Him, and it is true. وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ—however, most people have no knowledge of this reality.

translation: [It is] so He will make clear to them [the truth of] that wherein they differ and so those who have disbelieved may know that they were liars
commentary:
لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى يَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ كَـٰذِبِينَ

Allah ﷻ explains the purpose of resurrection: لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى يَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ—so He may clarify for them the things in which they used to differ. It will become clear on Judgment Day who stands where, who was right and who was wrong.

The ayah continues: وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ كَـٰذِبِينَ—and so that the disbelievers may come to know once and for all that they have been liars. This presents an interesting question: a liar already knows he's a liar, so how will he "get to know" he's a liar? You could say they will get to know they were wrong, because someone who's wrong may not realize their error. But why does Allah ﷻ use the term "liars" when the assumption is that they already know they're lying?

This perhaps speaks to a psychological phenomenon that occurs when someone lies for a long time. When you live with a lie for long enough, you start believing the lie yourself. It becomes a reality for you. You don't go back and check it anymore—you just adapt that identity, embrace it as your truth. On Judgment Day, it will come to them like a shocking realization: "Oh wait, that was wrong." That assumption they had ingrained deep inside their hearts—that was completely false all along.

translation: Indeed, Our word to a thing when We intend it is but that We say to it, "Be," and it is
commentary:
إِنَّمَا قَوْلُنَا لِشَىْءٍ إِذَآ أَرَدْنَـٰهُ أَن نَّقُولَ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ

This ayah presents the absolute power of Allah's ﷻ command: إِنَّمَا قَوْلُنَا لِشَىْءٍ إِذَآ أَرَدْنَـٰهُ—Our saying to something when We have intended it—أَن نَّقُولَ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ—all that it takes is that We say to it "Be," and it comes into existence.

That's all it takes. In other words, that Day of Judgment, that huge, momentous event they're denying—it's not far at all. It just takes one command, one "kun" (be) from Allah ﷻ, and it will be done. It will happen instantaneously.

translation: And those who emigrated for [the cause of] Allah after they had been wronged - We will surely settle them in this world in a good place; but the reward of the Hereafter is greater, if only they could know
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ فِى ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ظُلِمُوا۟ لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً ۖ وَلَأَجْرُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ

This ayah brings good news to those who migrated for Allah's ﷻ sake: وَٱلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ فِى ٱللَّهِ—those who have migrated for the sake of Allah with no goal before them except Allah—مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ظُلِمُوا۟—after they were wronged. Note the use of مَا here, which serves a grammatical function called masdariya, more common in classical Arabic, allowing the transition from "after" directly to the verb.

Allah ﷻ promises: لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً—We will settle them in this life in the most beautiful way. The word نُبَوِّئَ means to formally settle someone, to give them a stationary station, a home where they find everything suitable. This is addressing the early Muslims who had to leave Makkah, promising them that Allah ﷻ will give them stability, a place where they want to be.

The word تَبَوُّء relates to finding the right place, like a bird that finds the perfect branch to rest upon. Allah ﷻ will give the believers that sense of belonging and home. When Makkah was eventually conquered, the Muslims got to be wherever they wanted to be in their own homeland once again.

But the promise doesn't end there: وَلَأَجْرُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ—and the compensation of the afterlife is even greater. لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ—if only they had knowledge of what awaits them.

translation: [They are] those who endured patiently and upon their Lord relied
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ

This ayah describes the qualities of those who earned this promise: ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟—those who remained patient—وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ—and continuously they placed their trust, their complete reliance, only upon their Master.

These two qualities—sabr (patient perseverance) and tawakkul (trust in Allah ﷻ)—are what carried the early Muslims through their trials. They endured the persecution with patience, and they placed their complete trust in Allah ﷻ alone, not in worldly powers or material means.

translation: And We sent not before you except men to whom We revealed [Our message]. So ask the people of the message if you do not know
commentary:
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُّوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِمْ ۚ فَسْـَٔلُوٓا۟ أَهْلَ ٱلذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

Allah ﷻ addresses an objection from the Quraysh about the Prophet ﷺ being a human messenger: وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُّوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِمْ—We didn't send anyone before you except men to whom We revealed, to whom revelation came.

Then comes the command: فَسْـَٔلُوٓا۟ أَهْلَ ٱلذِّكْرِ—then ask the people of remembrance, the people of knowledge—إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ—if you yourselves have no idea. While this ayah is often used generally to mean "ask the people of knowledge if you don't know," the original context refers specifically to asking the People of the Book, particularly the scholars among the Jews and Christians.

This is significant because it shows that even their revelations were called dhikr (remembrance). Our revelation is also called dhikr in previous passages. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: ask the scholars, the rabbis, ask the knowledgeable among the Jews and Christians, and you'll find they're going to tell you about people who received revelation before. If you don't know about the prophets and the clear proofs and the scriptures that came before, go ask them and find out.

This opens a healthy conversation about how we should interact with the scholars of previous revelations. Historically, in many parts of the Muslim world, imams and scholars were expected to be knowledgeable about the Torah and Gospel, especially the lead imams. They were supposed to be knowledgeable in Latin and Greek, in addition to Arabic and all the Islamic sciences. This was standard expectation among scholars.

We're supposed to have an open, respectful interaction with sincere seekers of truth from other traditions. This is more important now than ever before. We have the good fortune now of some Muslims who have studied Christianity and Judaism in depth, and some Christian and Jewish scholars who have embraced Islam. We need to take advantage of those resources and start serious conversations about the common history mentioned in the Quran that's also found in the Torah and Gospel.

For instance, the story of Yusuf (AS) in the Bible is almost three times as long and includes details like how the minister had so much trust in Yusuf (AS) that he handed him the keys to the house and all the accounts for the vaults—the only thing he asked was to let him know when dinner was ready. That's how complete his trust was. These are biblical additions to the story that don't contradict what we have.

Many times you can learn beneficial things from Jewish and Christian sources. We don't take them as 100% authentic unless they're confirmed by our sources, but if they don't contradict our teachings and scripture, there's no harm in learning from them. Allah ﷻ mentions a lot of complex Jewish history, but He mentions it briefly and in an ambiguous fashion, focusing on the lesson itself rather than exhaustive historical detail. Often it's not in chronological order either—"Enter the city" (which city? where were the Jews? they were in the desert, needing to enter Jericho—what were the circumstances?).

Much of this detail originally came from the Sahaba who were People of the Book, who had learned about it in their scripture, then commented on it, and it became part of our tafsir tradition. But that was only one or two Sahaba who were learned in whatever limited form of Jewish or Christian studies was available to the Arabs. That means there's much more to explore in that world of scholarship. One day, insha'Allah, we will have those kinds of deep studies that really open the door to meaningful dialogue with knowledgeable people among the Christians and Jews.

One issue that's particularly troubling in modern times is that when you talk to some knowledgeable folks among the Jewish and Christian traditions about the prophets, they don't always speak about them with respect. They might describe them as "some guy who had these issues," attributing really horrible things to Ibrahim (AS) and other prophets. They claim these descriptions come from Scripture itself. We vehemently reject those kinds of allegations against the prophets. This is an important part of our role: cleaning up previous revelation, removing the impurities that were introduced into previous Scripture.

translation: [We sent them] with clear proofs and written ordinances. And We revealed to you the message that you may make clear to the people what was sent down to them and that they might give thought
commentary:
بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَٱلزُّبُرِ ۗ وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ مَا نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ

This ayah continues the thought: بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَٱلزُّبُرِ—with the clear proofs and the scriptures, the psalms of the past. If you don't have knowledge of previous revelations, go ask those who do.

Then Allah ﷻ addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly: وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلذِّكْرَ—and We have revealed to you the ultimate reminder—لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ مَا نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ—so that you may clarify for all people what was sent down to them. You've been given revelation so you can clarify for people what's in their previous scripture. Your scripture will qualify and clarify their scripture. That's what it will do.

وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ—and so that they may engage in deep thought, so they may think deeply about it. The Quran invites people to reflect, to think critically about the message they've received.

translation: Then, do those who have planned evil deeds feel secure that Allah will not cause the earth to swallow them or that the punishment will not come upon them from where they do not perceive
commentary:
أَفَأَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مَكَرُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ أَن يَخْسِفَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ issues a stern warning to those plotting against the believers: أَفَأَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مَكَرُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ—have those who are plotting all kinds of evils felt safe? أَن يَخْسِفَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ—that Allah will not sink the earth itself along with them in it? Not that Allah ﷻ will sink them into the earth—He will sink the earth itself with them in it.

أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ—or that punishment will come to them from where they couldn't have even realized, from a direction they never anticipated.

translation: Or that He would not seize them during their [usual] activity, and they could not cause failure
commentary:
أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ فِى تَقَلُّبِهِمْ فَمَا هُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ

The warning continues: أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ فِى تَقَلُّبِهِمْ—or He will seize them in the middle of their going about, their daily activities. They're going to the store, going back to work, just commuting through their busy day—"I've got stuff to do"—and in the middle of it all, Allah ﷻ will catch them.

فَمَا هُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ—then they won't be able to overpower or escape the punishment at all. They have no power to thwart Allah's ﷻ plan.

translation: Or that He would not seize them gradually [in a state of dread]? But indeed, your Lord is Kind and Merciful
commentary:
أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ عَلَىٰ تَخَوُّفٍ فَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ

أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ عَلَىٰ تَخَوُّفٍ—or He will seize them while they're on guard. The word تَخَوُّف comes from خوف (fear/caution), meaning they're on guard, waiting for the attack of the enemy, already ready for his attack. Even in this state of maximum alertness, Allah ﷻ says: I'll still get you. It doesn't matter how ready you think you are.

In the previous scenario, they were caught unaware doing other things. In this case, they were already on guard, scanning the horizon, trying to find out where the enemy will come from—and still, Allah's ﷻ punishment overtakes them.

Then comes the beautiful conclusion: فَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ—and certainly your Master is extremely compassionate, always merciful. This is striking after such stern warnings. The mercy? That He hasn't done this with you yet. That He's giving you time to turn back, to repent, to change your ways. His delaying the punishment is itself a manifestation of His mercy.

translation: Have they not considered what things Allah has created? Their shadows incline to the right and to the left, prostrating to Allah, while they are humble
commentary:
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ مِن شَىْءٍ يَتَفَيَّؤُا۟ ظِلَـٰلُهُۥ عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَٱلشَّمَآئِلِ سُجَّدًا لِّلَّهِ وَهُمْ دَٰخِرُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ shifts to a beautiful scene from nature: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ مِن شَىْءٍ—didn't they look to what Allah created of all kinds of things?

يَتَفَيَّؤُا۟ ظِلَـٰلُهُۥ—its shadows withdraw and pull back. The word تَفَيُّؤ comes from فَيْء, which means the shadow that returns or withdraws. As the day progresses, shadows trace back and pull away. عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَٱلشَّمَآئِلِ—they pull back from the right and the left, from the north and south.

سُجَّدًا لِّلَّهِ—in prostration to Allah. The shadows themselves are doing something like sujood, bowing down to Allah وَهُمْ دَٰخِرُونَ .ﷻ—and they, these creations of Allah ﷻ, are humbled, in a state of complete humility before Him.

Allah ﷻ is speaking about every tree, every creature—each one casts a shadow, and that shadow in and of itself is in prostration to Allah ﷻ. And that creature casting the shadow is itself in complete humility to Allah ﷻ.

The word دَاخِرُونَ combines three powerful meanings: humiliation (in the sense of being low, not prideful), embarrassment (recognition of one's smallness before the Creator), and powerlessness. These three concepts merge in this one word, describing the state of all creation before its Creator.

translation: And to Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth of creatures, and the angels [as well], and they are not arrogant
commentary:
وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِن دَآبَّةٍ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ

The declaration becomes even more universal: وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِن دَآبَّةٍ—and to Allah alone prostrates whatever is in the skies and whatever is in the earth of all creatures, every kind of creature.

وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ—and the angels do the same. وَهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ—and they don't show any arrogance whatsoever. There is no trace of pride or resistance in their worship. They submit completely.

translation: They fear their Lord above them, and they do what they are commanded
commentary:
يَخَافُونَ رَبَّهُم مِّن فَوْقِهِمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ مَا يُؤْمَرُونَ ۩

This final ayah of the section describes the attitude of the angels: يَخَافُونَ رَبَّهُم مِّن فَوْقِهِمْ—they fear their Master from above them. Despite being in the heavens, despite being noble creatures of light, they recognize Allah's ﷻ absolute authority above them. They maintain a conscious awareness of His power and majesty.

وَيَفْعَلُونَ مَا يُؤْمَرُونَ—and they do what they have been commanded. There is no delay, no questioning, no resistance. When Allah ﷻ commands, they obey immediately and completely.

The sajdah symbol (۩) at the end of this ayah indicates that this is one of the places in the Quran where we should make prostration when reciting or hearing it. We have been commanded to make sujood, to physically embody the submission that all of creation demonstrates in its own way. When we prostrate here, we join the universal symphony of worship—the shadows, the creatures, the angels—all humbling themselves before the One who created them.

translation: And Allah has said, "Do not take for yourselves two deities. He is but one God, so fear only Me
commentary:
۞ وَقَالَ ٱللَّهُ لَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ إِلَـٰهَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۖ إِنَّمَا هُوَ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ فَإِيَّـٰىَ فَٱرْهَبُونِ

Allah ﷻ declares: "Do not take two gods, two entities worthy of obedience and worship." The command is emphatic—لَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟, meaning "do not adopt" or "do not take for yourselves." The phrase إِلَـٰهَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ uses both the dual form and the number "two" for emphasis, driving home the absurdity of dividing worship between multiple deities.

This ayah addresses those who had distorted the concept of monotheism. Historical context shows that Christians often emphasized the Father and the Son in their theology, with less attention given to the Holy Spirit in their day-to-day worship. The focus became binary—father and son, father and son—creating a practical duality even within their trinitarian framework.

Allah ﷻ corrects this deviation with absolute clarity: إِنَّمَا هُوَ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ —"He is only one single entity worthy of worship and obedience." The restriction particle إِنَّمَا limits divinity to One alone.

The ayah concludes with فَإِيَّـٰىَ فَٱرْهَبُونِ —"Then be terrified, be extremely afraid only and only of Me." The construction emphasizes exclusivity: إِيَّـٰىَ means "Me alone," and the command فَٱرْهَبُونِ comes from رَهْبَة, denoting profound awe and fear. This isn't casual respect—it's the recognition that only Allah ﷻ deserves our ultimate reverence and submission. When we act with consciousness of His authority, our actions become witnesses that we truly fear Him.

translation: And to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and to Him is [due] worship constantly. Then is it other than Allah that you fear
commentary:
وَلَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَهُ ٱلدِّينُ وَاصِبًا ۚ أَفَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ تَتَّقُونَ

Everything in the heavens and the earth belongs to Allah ﷻ alone. But the ayah goes further: وَلَهُ ٱلدِّينُ وَاصِبًا —"And to Him alone, the religion belongs permanently and forever."

The word وَاصِبًا carries profound meaning. It comes from the root و-ص-ب which means permanent, unchanging, constant over time. The deen—the way of life, the religion, the divine guidance—belongs to Allah ﷻ in an eternal, unalterable way. The essential teachings of this religion will never change. They remain constant across time and communities.

This presents a powerful challenge: if the deen is unchanging, then any alterations found in religious practice must have come from human hands. The original teachings delivered to all prophets were consistent—monotheism, accountability, righteous living. Those who found contradictions and innovations in their inherited traditions were being confronted with an uncomfortable reality: they were the ones who had changed the religion, not Allah ﷻ.

The ayah concludes with a piercing question: أَفَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ تَتَّقُونَ —"Is it other than Allah that you fear?" This question strikes at the heart of human weakness. When sincere Christians and Jews would recognize truths in the Quran that resonated with the original, unchanged message, their next fear wouldn't be of Allah ﷻ—it would be social: What will my community say? What will my congregation think? What will happen to my position, my relationships, my identity?

Imagine a prominent religious leader with thousands of followers—someone whose voice reaches hundreds of thousands through media. The social cost of embracing truth becomes immense. But Allah ﷻ challenges this misplaced fear: Are you afraid of Allah ﷻ, or are you afraid of people?

translation: And whatever you have of favor - it is from Allah. Then when adversity touches you, to Him you cry for help
commentary:
وَمَا بِكُم مِّن نِّعْمَةٍ فَمِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِذَا مَسَّكُمُ ٱلضُّرُّ فَإِلَيْهِ تَجْـَٔرُونَ

Every blessing you enjoy—whatever comfort, luxury, or goodness you experience—comes from Allah ﷻ. The construction مَا بِكُم مِّن نِّعْمَةٍ فَمِنَ ٱللَّهِ uses the particle مِنْ to emphasize totality: any blessing whatsoever, regardless of its nature or size, has only one source.

But notice what happens when circumstances shift. ثُمَّ إِذَا مَسَّكُمُ ٱلضُّرُّ —"Then when harm touches you." The moment harm or difficulty arrives, what do humans do? فَإِلَيْهِ تَجْـَٔرُونَ —"You cry out to Him."

The word تَجْـَٔرُونَ comes from جُؤَار, which describes the desperate, anguished cry of an animal under attack or starving—raw, primal, unfiltered. Allah ﷻ describes people in crisis as returning to their animal nature, making desperate sounds of pain and need, all directed toward Him. In that moment of genuine need, all pretense falls away. The idols are forgotten, the intermediaries are bypassed, the philosophical doubts evaporate. It's straight to Allah ﷻ.

This pattern reveals something profound about human nature: deep down, everyone knows who truly has power. In moments of genuine crisis, the heart returns to its natural state of recognizing the One.

translation: Then when He removes the adversity from you, at once a party of you associates others with their Lord
commentary:
ثُمَّ إِذَا كَشَفَ ٱلضُّرَّ عَنكُمْ إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنكُم بِرَبِّهِمْ يُشْرِكُونَ

كَشَفَ means to alleviate, to remove, to lift away. When Allah ﷻ removes the harm and difficulty, when the crisis passes and relief comes—what happens?

إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنكُم بِرَبِّهِمْ يُشْرِكُونَ —"Immediately, a group among you starts making associations with their Master." The word إِذَا indicates instantaneous action. As soon as the relief comes, without pause or reflection, some people immediately return to shirk—attributing their rescue to other causes, other beings, other forces.

The pronoun shift is striking: بِرَبِّهِمْ —"with their Master." Even in describing their betrayal, Allah ﷻ reminds them of the relationship. He is still their Master, their Rabb, their Lord—even as they commit shirk against Him.

This pattern—turning to Allah ﷻ in crisis, then turning away in comfort—represents one of the most common forms of spiritual ingratitude. It reveals hearts that haven't truly submitted, that use Allah ﷻ as a emergency contact rather than recognizing Him as the constant source of all conditions, both difficult and easy.

translation: So they will deny what We have given them. Then enjoy yourselves, for you are going to know
commentary:
لِيَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ ۚ فَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ ۖ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ

لِيَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ —"So that they may disbelieve in what We have given them." The phrase drips with sarcasm. Let them deny everything We've provided. Let them persist in their ingratitude and shirk.

But notice the grammatical shift: the ayah begins in third person—"they should go ahead and disbelieve"—then suddenly shifts to second person: فَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ —"So you all enjoy yourselves."

This shift from third to second person creates a chilling effect. Imagine a teacher addressing a class: "Students can just walk in late whenever they feel like it. They can leave class whenever they want." Third person—distant, observational. Then suddenly: "So go ahead, you all enjoy yourselves. You'll find out the consequences very soon."

The direct address is confrontational, personal, threatening. It eliminates distance and forces awareness. The verse concludes: فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ —"And you will know. Very soon, you will know." The particle سَوْفَ indicates near-future certainty. This isn't a vague threat about a distant judgment day—this is imminent. The consequences are coming, and they will be undeniable.

translation: And they assign to what they do not know a portion of that which We have provided them. By Allah, you will surely be questioned about what you used to invent
commentary:
وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُونَ نَصِيبًا مِّمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ ۗ تَٱللَّهِ لَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ عَمَّا كُنتُمْ تَفْتَرُونَ

They set aside a portion—نَصِيبًا —of what Allah ﷻ has provided them for deities about which they have no knowledge. لِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُونَ —"for what they do not know." They're making religious dedications and sacrifices to entities they cannot even properly identify or understand. Their ignorance is compounded by their confidence.

From what Allah ﷻ has provided them—مِّمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ —they allocate portions to imaginary beings. The irony is devastating: Allah ﷻ provides the sustenance, and they use that very sustenance to honor false gods.

Then comes an oath of unprecedented intensity: تَٱللَّهِ —"I swear by Allah." This form appears only twice in the Quran, and both times in situations of extreme gravity. Previously, Allah ﷻ swore by the Prophet ﷺ regarding the madness of the people of Prophet Nuh (AS). Now He swears by Himself regarding the crimes of the Quraysh.

لَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ عَمَّا كُنتُمْ تَفْتَرُونَ —"You will absolutely, certainly be interrogated about the things you have been fabricating." The emphasis is unmistakable. The accountability will be thorough, detailed, and inescapable. Every fabrication, every lie attributed to religion, every innovation falsely claimed as divine—all of it will be exposed and accounted for.

translation: And they attribute to Allah daughters - exalted is He - and for them is what they desire
commentary:
وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ ٱلْبَنَـٰتِ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۙ وَلَهُم مَّا يَشْتَهُونَ

They attribute daughters to Allah ﷻ. The immediate response: سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ —"Perfect is He, far exalted is He above such attribution." The declaration of Allah's perfection serves as an instant refutation of this blasphemy.

وَلَهُم مَّا يَشْتَهُونَ —"And for themselves they have what they desire"—meaning sons. They reserve for themselves what they consider valuable and desirable, while attributing to Allah ﷻ what they consider inferior.

This isn't just a historical issue limited to pre-Islamic Arabia. The preference for sons over daughters—this cultural disease—appears immediately after Allah ﷻ mentions shirk and blasphemy. The positioning is deliberate: gender-based discrimination and preference are being directly associated with grave theological crimes.

This message needs to be heard clearly across Muslim communities: South Asian communities—Indian, Pakistani, Bangladeshi—where this prejudice runs deep; Indonesian, Egyptian, Moroccan, Somali communities wherever this attitude persists. The Quran places this crime right alongside shirk. This isn't a minor cultural quirk to be tolerated—it's a spiritual disease that Allah ﷻ condemns in the strongest terms.

The logic people used was purely materialistic: a son provides support, backup, protection. He strengthens the family's position. A daughter is viewed as a liability—she requires protection, she'll eventually marry into another family, she takes a portion of inheritance with her. It's expense after expense. Better to have sons who can support me. This mercenary calculation toward children is precisely what Allah ﷻ dismantles in the following ayahs.

translation: And when one of them is informed of [the birth of] a female, his face becomes dark, and he suppresses grief
commentary:
وَإِذَا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُم بِٱلْأُنثَىٰ ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُۥ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ

إِذَا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُم بِٱلْأُنثَىٰ —"When one of them is given the good news of a female child." The word بُشِّرَ comes from بِشَارَة —good news, glad tidings. The irony is built into the word choice: what should be received as good news becomes a source of devastation.

ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُۥ مُسْوَدًّا —"His face remains darkened." The verb ظَلَّ means to remain, to stay in a state. This isn't a momentary expression—his face stays dark. The darkness isn't merely metaphorical; in their culture, the visible transformation of someone's expression communicated their inner state to everyone around them.

وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ —"And he is suppressing, swallowing his rage and sadness." كَظِيمٌ comes from كَظْم, meaning to swallow something large and difficult, to suppress intense emotion. He's choking on his disappointment, barely containing his anger and grief—all because a daughter was born.

This description should shock us. The birth of a child—any child—is a moment of profound blessing. Yet here is a father whose face darkens, who can barely speak, who is consumed with suppressed rage, all because the child is female.

When Hudhayfah (RA) would recite these ayahs, he would cry uncontrollably—because before accepting Islam, this is exactly what he had done. The shame of his pre-Islamic attitudes would overwhelm him. The Quran's preservation of these ayahs serves as a perpetual reminder that this attitude is not merely cultural—it's a manifestation of jahiliyyah, ignorance, that must be actively opposed in every generation.

translation: He hides himself from the people because of the ill of which he has been informed. Should he keep it in humiliation or bury it in the ground? Unquestionably, evil is what they decide
commentary:
يَتَوَٰرَىٰ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ مِن سُوٓءِ مَا بُشِّرَ بِهِۦٓ ۚ أَيُمْسِكُهُۥ عَلَىٰ هُونٍ أَمْ يَدُسُّهُۥ فِى ٱلتُّرَابِ ۗ أَلَا سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ

يَتَوَٰرَىٰ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ —"He hides himself from the people." The word يَتَوَٰرَىٰ (yatawara) specifically means to hide after being defeated or humiliated. Picture someone who lost badly at a competition and retreats to lock themselves away, unwilling to face anyone. That's the emotional state being described.

مِن سُوٓءِ مَا بُشِّرَ بِهِۦٓ —"Because of the 'evil' of what he was given as 'good news.'" The quotes are implied—what should be good news, he receives as evil news. He's too humiliated to show his face in public because a daughter was born.

His mind races with a horrific calculation: أَيُمْسِكُهُۥ عَلَىٰ هُونٍ أَمْ يَدُسُّهُۥ فِى ٱلتُّرَابِ —"Should he keep her despite the humiliation, or should he bury her in the dirt?"

The word هُونٍ means disgrace, humiliation, shame. Should he keep this child and continue living with what he perceives as disgrace? أَمْ —"or"—should he bury her? The verb يَدُسُّهُۥ means to bury, to push into the earth. This is the practice of وَأْد البَنَات —female infanticide—that was practiced in pre-Islamic Arabia.

أَلَا سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ—"What a horrible, evil judgment they are making!" This isn't a neutral life choice—this is يَحْكُمُونَ , a judgment, a decision being rendered. And it's utterly evil.

The Quran's detailed description of this father's psychology—his darkened face, his suppressed rage, his hiding from people, his calculated deliberation about whether to keep or kill his daughter—all of this detail serves to expose and condemn the attitude at its root. The problem isn't just the act of infanticide; the problem begins with viewing a daughter as a source of shame.

translation: For those who do not believe in the Hereafter is the description of evil; and for Allah is the highest attribute. And He is Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مَثَلُ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۖ وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْمَثَلُ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مَثَلُ ٱلسَّوْءِ —"For those who do not believe in the Akhirah belongs the worst example."

This placement is crucial. Allah ﷻ describes people who don't believe in the afterlife immediately after describing those with discriminatory attitudes toward daughters. The connection? Someone who views daughters as worthless or burdensome must not truly believe in accountability. If they believed they would stand before Allah ﷻ and answer for their attitudes and actions toward their children, how could they maintain such prejudice?

The worst examples—the worst attitudes, the worst behaviors, the worst characteristics—these belong to people who have disconnected from belief in ultimate accountability.

وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْمَثَلُ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ —"And to Allah belongs the highest example." This phrase operates on multiple levels. First, when Allah ﷻ describes Himself and His relationship with creation, He uses the highest, most perfect examples. The parables and comparisons in the Quran are pedagogically flawless.

Second, the relationships Allah ﷻ has with us can be compared to certain human relationships—but only to illuminate a point, and always with the recognition that Allah's reality transcends any comparison. For instance, Allah's mercy can be compared to a mother's love for her child, and indeed the word الرَّحِيمُ shares a root with رَحِم, the womb. But Allah's mercy is infinite and perfect, while a mother's mercy is finite and imperfect.

The ayah concludes: وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ—"And He is the Almighty, the All-Wise." العَزِيزُ denotes ultimate authority, overwhelming power, unassailable might. الحَكِيمُ indicates perfect wisdom—every action, every decree, every allowance, every prohibition stems from complete knowledge and perfect wisdom.

translation: And if Allah were to impose blame on the people for their wrongdoing, He would not have left upon the earth any creature, but He defers them for a specified term. And when their term has come, they will not remain behind an hour, nor will they precede [it]
commentary:
وَلَوْ يُؤَاخِذُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّاسَ بِظُلْمِهِم مَّا تَرَكَ عَلَيْهَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ سَاعَةً ۖ وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ

وَلَوْ يُؤَاخِذُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّاسَ بِظُلْمِهِم —"And if Allah were to seize people for their wrongdoing..." The verb يُؤَاخِذُ means to take to account, to seize, to grab in accountability.

Which wrongdoing particularly was just mentioned? Shirk was mentioned. The attitude of discrimination against daughters was mentioned. But the statement is general: بِظُلْمِهِم —"for their wrongdoing," any and all injustice.

مَّا تَرَكَ عَلَيْهَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ —"He would not leave upon it a single creature that walks." If Allah ﷻ enacted immediate justice for every act of oppression and injustice, no living being would remain on earth. The totality is emphasized: مِن دَآبَّةٍ —"not even one walking creature."

This reveals the staggering extent of human wrongdoing. Every moment of every day, countless acts of injustice occur. If immediate punishment followed each act, life itself would cease.

وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى —"However, He gives them respite until a deadline that is named." يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ  means He grants delay, He gives extra time. أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى —"a named deadline," an appointed term that is fixed and known to Allah ﷻ.

This respite is mercy. It's an opportunity for repentance, for change, for return. But it's not infinite.

فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ —"But when their deadline arrives..." The particle فَإِذَا indicates immediate consequence.

لَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ سَاعَةً ۖ وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ —"They cannot delay it by a single hour, nor can they bring it forward."

Why mention bringing it forward? Because when the questioning and accountability begin, when the reality of judgment becomes apparent, some people will wish for it to end. The intensity of questioning will be so overwhelming that they'll want to rush to whatever comes next. But they can't. لَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ —they cannot bring the end forward. They must experience the full weight of accountability.
