translation: And they attribute to Allah that which they dislike, and their tongues assert the lie that they will have the best [from Him]. Assuredly, they will have the Fire, and they will be [therein] neglected
commentary:
وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ مَا يَكْرَهُونَ وَتَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُهُمُ ٱلْكَذِبَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۖ لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ ٱلنَّارَ وَأَنَّهُم مُّفْرَطُونَ

وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ مَا يَكْرَهُونَ —"And they attribute to Allah what they themselves hate." They assign to Allah ﷻ what they would never accept for themselves—daughters they consider inferior, attributes they find unworthy, characteristics they despise.

وَتَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُهُمُ ٱلْكَذِبَ —"And their tongues describe, attribute, and fabricate lies." أَلْسِنَتُهُمُ —"their tongues," the instruments of their lies. The plural suggests widespread, repeated, communal lying.

أَنَّ لَهُمُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ —"That for themselves is the best ending." Despite their injustice, their shirk, their lies, they claim they'll have الْحُسْنَىٰ —the best possible outcome, the ultimate good, Paradise.

لَا جَرَمَ —"Without doubt, certainly, absolutely." This phrase emphasizes absolute certainty, removing any possibility of ambiguity.

أَنَّ لَهُمُ ٱلنَّارَ —"That for them is the Fire." Not Paradise, not the best ending, but the Fire.

وَأَنَّهُم مُّفْرَطُونَ —"And that they will be abandoned, left, forgotten." The word مُّفْرَطُونَ comes from فَرَطَ, meaning to be left behind, abandoned, forgotten. Remember the father considering burying his daughter, thinking he could forget about her and abandon her? These people will be مُّفْرَطُونَ—left in the Fire, abandoned, forgotten, just as they were willing to abandon and forget their own daughters.

The justice is poetic and precise.

translation: By Allah, We did certainly send [messengers] to nations before you, but Satan made their deeds attractive to them. And he is the disbelievers' ally today [as well], and they will have a painful punishment
commentary:
تَٱللَّهِ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

تَٱللَّهِ —"I swear by Allah." Again, this strongest form of oath, used sparingly in the Quran. The intensity of the oath reflects the gravity of what's being conveyed.

لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ —"We certainly sent messengers to nations before you." This isn't new. The pattern is established. Prophet after prophet, nation after nation, the same message delivered, the same rejection experienced.

فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ —"And Shaytan beautified for them their deeds." زَيَّنَ means to beautify, to make attractive, to decorate and embellish. Shaytan's primary tactic isn't to make evil appear evil—people would reject it. His strategy is to make evil appear good, to make wrong appear right, to make harmful appear beneficial.

Their deeds—أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ —their actions, their practices, their beliefs, their attitudes (like the discrimination against daughters)—all of it was made to appear beautiful, justified, even righteous in their eyes.

فَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ —"So he is their protective friend, their guardian, their close ally today." وَلِيّ indicates close friendship, protective guardianship, loyal alliance. They chose Shaytan as their waliyy, and they got what they chose.

وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ —"And for them is painful punishment." أَلِيمٌ means painful, causing deep suffering. The punishment matches the crime—they inflicted pain through their injustices, and they will experience pain as consequence.

translation: And We have not revealed to you the Book, [O Muhammad], except for you to make clear to them that wherein they have differed and as guidance and mercy for a people who believe
commentary:
وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّا لِتُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ ۙ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ

وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّا —"And We have not sent down the Book to you except..." The restriction particle إِلَّا limits the purpose to what follows.

لِتُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ —"So that you may clarify thoroughly for them that which they disagreed about." The verb تُبَيِّنَ comes from بَيَان, meaning clear explanation, thorough clarification. This is the primary function of the Quran—to resolve the theological disputes, to clarify the distortions, to restore the original message.

People had disagreed about fundamental matters: the nature of Allah ﷻ, the purpose of life, the reality of resurrection, the requirements of righteousness. The Quran came to cut through the confusion and provide definitive clarity.

وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً —"And as guidance and mercy." These are additional purposes. هُدًى —guidance, showing the path. رَحْمَةً —mercy, divine compassion manifested in the gift of clear direction.

لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ —"For a people who want to believe, who are willing to believe." The present tense verb يُؤْمِنُونَ indicates ongoing willingness. The Quran's guidance and mercy are available, but they require receptivity. Those who approach with sincerity and openness will find what they seek.

translation: And Allah has sent down rain from the sky and given life thereby to the earth after its lifelessness. Indeed in that is a sign for a people who listen
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَآ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ

وَٱللَّهُ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً —"And Allah sent down water from the sky." The same verb أَنزَلَ used for revealing the Quran is used here for sending rain. The parallel is intentional.

فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَآ —"And He gave life to the earth by means of it, after its death." The earth was dead—dry, barren, lifeless. The water descends, penetrates the soil, and suddenly life emerges. Seeds germinate, plants grow, color returns, vitality spreads.

This is the physical reality. But the ayah points to a deeper parallel.

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ —"Indeed, in that is a miraculous sign for people who really listen."

Why specifically "who listen"? Because the sign isn't just about water and plants. The revelation—the Quran—descends from above like rain. It encounters hearts that have become hard, dry, barren, lifeless. If those hearts truly listen, if they receive and absorb the words of Allah ﷻ, then life returns. The heart softens, consciousness awakens, purpose emerges, spiritual vitality flourishes.

But just as rain only benefits earth that receives it—not rock that repels it—revelation only transforms hearts that truly listen. يَسْمَعُونَ doesn't mean merely hearing sounds. It means listening with intent to understand, receiving with willingness to be affected, absorbing with readiness to change.

For those who listen in this way, the Quran becomes rain for their hearts, bringing dead souls back to life.

May Allah ﷻ make us among those who truly listen, who receive His revelation with open hearts, and who allow it to bring life to what has become hard and lifeless within us.

translation: And indeed, for you in grazing livestock is a lesson. We give you drink from what is in their bellies - between excretion and blood - pure milk, palatable to drinkers
commentary:
**وَإِنَّ لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ لَعِبْرَةً ۖ نُّسْقِيكُم مِّمَّا فِى بُطُونِهِۦ مِنۢ بَيْنِ فَرْثٍ وَدَمٍ لَّبَنًا خَالِصًا سَآئِغًا لِّلشَّـٰرِبِينَ**

We arrive at ayah 66 of Surah An-Nahl, which marks nearly the halfway point of this remarkable surah. As we continue through this passage, we encounter a comprehensive list of Allah's ﷻ favors upon humanity. Embedded within this enumeration are profound philosophical arguments that represent the culmination of the Makkan discourse. These are essentially the concluding remarks of the Makkan arguments, as the following surahs will introduce a different kind of Quranic education drawing heavily from history and human behavior.

The central theme of this portion of the surah revolves around reflection on creation. We are commanded to pay attention to the world around us and think deeply about what most people take for granted. This is one of the defining characteristics of the Quranic mindset. Someone who has truly internalized the thought process of the Quran doesn't see the world the same way as others do. The very same things that remain mundane to everyone else become profound points of reflection for the person immersed in Quranic thinking.

Allah ﷻ says: "And indeed, for you in cattle there is a powerful lesson." The word عِبْرَة carries deep significance. It comes from عَبَرَ, meaning to cross over something. An ibrah is literally a lesson that makes tears cross over the eye—a lesson so powerful it should move you to tears. Consider this: the Arab is being told to look at the cow and become so lost in thought that he is moved to tears. Why would the Arab feel this way?

To understand this, we must place ourselves in the desert context. The Arab finds himself surrounded by death, with no sustenance and severe scarcity of water. The biggest challenge in a desert climate is finding something to drink. One of the fundamental sources of both drink and nourishment for the Arab is milk.

Allah ﷻ says: "We give you drink." Notice that He ﷻ could have said "the cattle gives you drink," but instead He says "We give you drink"—taking direct credit for this provision. نُّسْقِيكُم مِّمَّا فِى بُطُونِهِۦ "from what is in its belly." Then comes the miraculous detail: مِنۢ بَيْنِ فَرْثٍ وَدَمٍ "from between filth and blood." You draw the milk from the cow's udders, yet if you were to slaughter that same cow, you wouldn't find any milk stored inside. Somehow, miraculously, it keeps producing pure milk.

لَّبَنًا خَالِصًا —pure milk, with no blood in it, no feces in it. Then Allah ﷻ describes it as سَآئِغًا لِّلشَّـٰرِبِينَ —that goes down easy for those who drink. The word سَآئِغ describes a drink that goes down so smoothly you don't want to put it down. You keep gulping it, wanting more and more. This is the milk that Allah ﷻ provides, and it applies across the board—people universally enjoy this drink.

translation: And from the fruits of the palm trees and grapevines you take intoxicant and good provision. Indeed in that is a sign for a people who reason
commentary:
**وَمِن ثَمَرَٰتِ ٱلنَّخِيلِ وَٱلْأَعْنَـٰبِ تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَرًا وَرِزْقًا حَسَنًا ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ**

"And from the fruits of palm trees and grapevines, you draw out from it all kinds of drinks and good provisions." The word سَكَر means wine but also refers to sweet drinks. Here, Allah ﷻ makes an interesting observation that will be elaborated later in this surah: He takes credit even for human creativity.

When Allah ﷻ speaks about the tree, the cow, and the milk, we acknowledge these as entirely from Him—they have nothing to do with human design. But now consider what happens when you take dates and grapes and create a special dish, perhaps a pudding with grapes in it or some other creative recipe. You might say, "This is my creation, this is my innovation." Yet Allah ﷻ says even that رِزْقًا حَسَنًا —good provision—ultimately goes back to His credit.

This reveals something profound about the Muslim mindset. We don't simply appreciate that certain things are Allah's ﷻ creation and serve as reminders of Him. Rather, the computer, the iPad, the camera, the light bulb—these too become manifestations of Allah's ﷻ signs because they are products of what He ﷻ Himself created: the human mind and the natural ingredients necessary to assemble these inventions.

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ —"Truly, there is a miraculous sign in that for a nation that tries to understand." Note the use of the verb form يَعْقِلُونَ rather than the noun عَاقِلُون. When ya'qiloon appears as a verb, it indicates an attempt being made—a nation that attempts to understand, that tries to comprehend. For such people, these become miraculous signs.

translation: And your Lord inspired to the bee, "Take for yourself among the mountains, houses, and among the trees and [in] that which they construct
commentary:
**وَأَوْحَىٰ رَبُّكَ إِلَى ٱلنَّحْلِ أَنِ ٱتَّخِذِى مِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ بُيُوتًا وَمِنَ ٱلشَّجَرِ وَمِمَّا يَعْرِشُونَ**

Now we come to the namesake of this surah: the bee. وَأَوْحَىٰ رَبُّكَ إِلَى ٱلنَّحْلِ —"And your Master revealed to the bee." The use of وَحْي (revelation) here is striking. Allah ﷻ reveals to this tiny creature أَنِ ٱتَّخِذِى مِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ بُيُوتًا —"that you should take from mountains homes."

This tiny bee is going to make homes where human beings struggle to build. Remember the previous nations destroyed in the past? When Allah ﷻ elaborated on their power, He mentioned how they carved homes out of mountains. Now this tiny creature is commanded to do the same—to make homes even in the mountains.

وَمِنَ ٱلشَّجَرِ —"and from trees." The bee builds its hive among tree branches. وَمِمَّا يَعْرِشُونَ —"and in the buildings that they raise." The word عَرَش refers to something elevated, something built high. When human beings construct buildings, putting beams together and creating arches, the bee finds some corner and builds its hive there too. To this day, we have exterminators coming to skyscrapers to remove beehives on the thirtieth floor of a building.

translation: Then eat from all the fruits and follow the ways of your Lord laid down [for you]." There emerges from their bellies a drink, varying in colors, in which there is healing for people. Indeed in that is a sign for a people who give thought
commentary:
**ثُمَّ كُلِى مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ فَٱسْلُكِى سُبُلَ رَبِّكِ ذُلُلًا ۚ يَخْرُجُ مِنۢ بُطُونِهَا شَرَابٌ مُّخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوَٰنُهُۥ فِيهِ شِفَآءٌ لِّلنَّاسِ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ**

ثُمَّ كُلِى مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ —"Then consume from all kinds of fruit, all manner of fruit." The command continues: فَٱسْلُكِى سُبُلَ رَبِّكِ ذُلُلًا —"Then pursue the pathways of your Master, in complete submission."

The word سَلَكَ means to pursue a path, to go down a road. If you ever observe a bee moving, it doesn't travel in a straight line. It goes in spirals, taking seemingly random directions. When you throw an object, it travels straight, but the bee moves in multiple pathways, taking turn after turn. That's why the plural سُبُل (pathways) is used. Every direction becomes a path as the bee takes one turn, then another, then another.

What makes this remarkable is that Allah ﷻ describes every one of these navigational turns as something He dictated through revelation. The bee is following divine guidance. Modern studies of bee behavior reveal that when bees take these spiral paths, they're meeting air resistance, and they're actually engineered to take these turns to follow the path of least resistance. They're designed to take the most efficient path for their size, given the air friction they face.

The word ذُلُلًا comes from ذَلَّ, meaning powerless or humbled. When someone puts their head down in humility, that's also called dhull. We encountered this word before when describing cattle: ذَلُولٌ —a cow that has been domesticated, its head put down so it does what people tell it to do. But the bee hasn't been domesticated by humans. Rather, Allah ﷻ tells us this bee is in complete submission and humility to Him. The path it takes is one of humble obedience.

Before continuing with the ayah, consider the purpose of these signs of creation. They exist for our reflection. Next time you see a bee, remember that it follows revelation from Allah ﷻ. Let it become a reminder for you and me to follow revelation. Allah ﷻ commands this creature directly—what to eat, where to go, how to walk, how to fly, what path to take. This should prompt us to ask: What path am I taking?

يَخْرُجُ مِنۢ بُطُونِهَا شَرَابٌ —"A drink comes out from its very belly." Now we have two bellies mentioned. The first was the cow's belly that gave us milk. Now Allah ﷻ tells us there's another belly—the belly of this tiny creature.

مُّخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوَٰنُهُۥ —"different are its textures." Notice the structure here: the صِفَة (descriptor) comes before the مَوْصُوف (the thing being described). This construction emphasizes: "How different its textures are!" You have different forms of honey. If you visit stores that sell natural honey without preservatives, you'll see completely different textures. Local honey from one region differs vastly from local honey from another region.

فِيهِ شِفَآءٌ لِّلنَّاسِ —"In it is healing for people." The drink from the cow goes down easily and provides sustenance. The drink provided by this tiny creature, by the command of Allah ﷻ, contains healing properties.

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ —"And in all of that, truly there is a miraculous sign for a nation that wants to think deeply." Look at the progression of verbs. We went from يَعْقِلُونَ (they try to understand) to يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (they think deeply). تَفَكُّر represents deeper thought than عَقْل. Why this progression?

Consider what's easier to observe: milk coming from a cow or honey coming from a bee? Observing milk from a cow is straightforward—the process is direct. You simply milk the udder and milk comes out. But the process by which honey is derived—what the bee goes through, the pathways it takes to reach flowers, extract nectar, return to the hive, and produce honey from its belly—this requires much deeper contemplation.

translation: And Allah created you; then He will take you in death. And among you is he who is reversed to the most decrepit [old] age so that he will not know, after [having had] knowledge, a thing. Indeed, Allah is Knowing and Competent
commentary:
**وَٱللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ يَتَوَفَّىٰكُمْ ۚ وَمِنكُم مَّن يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَرْذَلِ ٱلْعُمُرِ لِكَىْ لَا يَعْلَمَ بَعْدَ عِلْمٍ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ قَدِيرٌ**

Allah ﷻ now turns to address us directly: وَٱللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ يَتَوَفَّىٰكُمْ —"Allah created you, then He will take you away." The transition here is powerful. In the previous ayah, Allah ﷻ mentioned شِفَآء (cure, healing) for people. But now He reminds us: no cure exists for your death. You will die, and there will be no healing from that.

There will be no procedure, no surgery, no treatment that can prevent you from getting old. This is a beautiful transition—first, cure is mentioned, then we're told that regardless of any cure, there are certain things from which we will never be healed: death and old age.

وَمِنكُم مَّن يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَرْذَلِ ٱلْعُمُرِ —"And among you are those who will be returned to the most feeble, despicable age." The word أَرْذَل comes from رَذْل, meaning weak, despicable, beneath everything. Allah ﷻ will let some of you reach that state of weakness where you might wish for death, but your time hasn't come yet. You reach extreme old age and deterioration.

لِكَىْ لَا يَعْلَمَ بَعْدَ عِلْمٍ شَيْـًٔا —"So that he may not know, after having knowledge, anything at all." This is the Quranic expression for senility. His memory lapses. He can't retain anything.

Recently, I had the opportunity to meet a great scholar in his late seventies. We were traveling together, and I deeply admire his work and his talks. We were in the car—he was in the front, I was in the back, with a brother driving. We started talking, and he asked, "Where are you from?" I said, "Dallas." "How long have you been there?" "Three years." "What's your name again?" "Nouman." "Oh, yes..."

Then, about thirty seconds later: "So where are you from?" "Dallas." "How long have you been there?" He repeated this conversation three times. It almost moved me to tears. This is a scholar—someone who knows so much—yet laa ya'lama ba'da ilmin shay'a. This will happen to any one of us. None of us are immune from it.

In another place in the Quran, in Surah Ya-Sin, Allah ﷻ describes this as being "reversed in creation." Some of our limitations from childhood start returning when we get old. Who repeats conversations over and over? A child does. Who keeps forgetting? "Don't touch that." And the child touches it anyway. "I forgot." Does it again. When we reach extreme old age, aspects of childhood return.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ قَدِيرٌ —"Indeed, Allah is All-Knowing and All-Powerful." What a beautiful conclusion to this ayah. Allah's ﷻ knowledge doesn't deteriorate. Allah's ﷻ capability doesn't deteriorate. Yours does. Your knowledge deteriorates, your capability deteriorates. The contrast is made explicit.

translation: And Allah has favored some of you over others in provision. But those who were favored would not hand over their provision to those whom their right hands possess so they would be equal to them therein. Then is it the favor of Allah they reject
commentary:
**وَٱللَّهُ فَضَّلَ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فِى ٱلرِّزْقِ ۚ فَمَا ٱلَّذِينَ فُضِّلُوا۟ بِرَآدِّى رِزْقِهِمْ عَلَىٰ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ فَهُمْ فِيهِ سَوَآءٌ ۚ أَفَبِنِعْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ**

وَٱللَّهُ فَضَّلَ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فِى ٱلرِّزْقِ —"And Allah has given preference to some of you over others in provision." Age itself is a form of provision. Someone lives seventeen years, someone twenty-seven years, someone one hundred and seven years. That too is rizq. So Allah ﷻ says not everyone will reach the same age, and depending on your age, you'll learn different things and receive different provisions.

This ayah addresses a fundamental question about economic disparity. When Muslims engaged with communist ideology in the past century, communists argued that the disparity of wealth—some being wealthy while others are poor—is not a result of divine wisdom but rather of false economic systems like capitalism. They claimed greedy people get more while others are left behind, and therefore we need a justice system based on equality where everyone receives the same salary—the doctor and the bus driver earning identically.

The Quran teaches something different. We are not created the same way. Some of us are naturally smarter in certain areas than others. Some are physically capable, others intellectually gifted. Some think concretely and tangibly, others abstractly. Some excel at mathematics, others at creativity. We have vastly different aptitudes, capabilities, and situations.

Therefore, there will be disparity in wealth. Islamic economic philosophy doesn't mandate that everyone make exactly the same income. There will be economic diversity—people in the Ummah who are doing well and others who are struggling. What Islam mandates is a welfare system in which those at the bottom still live a decent life because they're provided for by the rest of society. Their minimum needs are met. The orphan, the widow, the weak, the poor, the helpless—all are taken care of through various mechanisms.

Now comes the powerful comparison: فَمَا ٱلَّذِينَ فُضِّلُوا۟ بِرَآدِّى رِزْقِهِمْ عَلَىٰ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ فَهُمْ فِيهِ سَوَآءٌ —"Those who have been blessed with wealth are never going to give back their provisions to their slaves, to what their right hands possess, making them equal."

In modern terms, you're a millionaire with servants, secretaries, security guards, drivers. مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ literally refers to slaves, but today we can think of employees and hired help. You receive income from your business. Would you ever think, "I should take my entire salary and give exactly half to my secretary or my driver or my security guard, making us equal"? No businessman or executive would ever think this way. This thought would never cross their mind.

The word used is particularly striking: مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ—"what their right hands possess," meaning slaves. Even if you consider promoting an employee, there's one job description from which you never get promoted: slavery. At most, you might consider freeing a slave, releasing them from obligation, but making them your equal? You would never consider that. فَهُمْ فِيهِ سَوَآءٌ —"so that they're equal with you."

Here's the profound point: You detest the very idea of taking someone lower than you—someone you actually own—and making them your equal. That thought never crosses your mind. Yet when it comes to your life decisions, you somehow think you have as much authority as Allah ﷻ. You think you should be on the same level as your Creator. You give Allah ﷻ some of your time and some worship, but the rest is your own decision, as though you have partnership with Him.

أَفَبِنِعْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ —"Is it then the favor of Allah that they're rejecting?" The great favor being referenced is that He ﷻ hasn't yet brought His punishment upon them despite their arrogance. This rhetorical question drives home the absurdity of human pride in the face of divine authority.

translation: And Allah has made for you from yourselves mates and has made for you from your mates sons and grandchildren and has provided for you from the good things. Then in falsehood do they believe and in the favor of Allah they disbelieve
commentary:
**وَٱللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُم بَنِينَ وَحَفَدَةً وَرَزَقَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ ۚ أَفَبِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ يُؤْمِنُونَ وَبِنِعْمَتِ ٱللَّهِ هُمْ يَكْفُرُونَ**

Allah ﷻ begins this ayah by reminding us of blessings that extend far beyond our individual lifespans. He says, "Allah has made for you from among yourselves spouses." This blessing of companionship from your own kind—human beings married to human beings—is the foundation of family life.

But the blessings don't stop there. The phrase وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُم بَنِينَ وَحَفَدَةً tells us that from these spouses come children and grandchildren. The word حَفَدَة refers to grandchildren, highlighting how Allah's ﷻ blessings ripple through generations. When you look at your own children, this is a favor from Allah ﷻ. When you see your grandchildren, this too is His favor.

Then Allah ﷻ mentions, وَرَزَقَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ —"And He has provided you from all manner of good and pure things." This encompasses every wholesome provision: food, shelter, comfort and all the good things we enjoy in life.

After listing these tremendous blessings, Allah ﷻ poses a jarring question: أَفَبِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ يُؤْمِنُونَ —"Is it then in falsehood that they believe?" What could possibly be so compelling about falsehood that even when you look at your own children, they become evidence you ignore? Even your grandchildren become blessings you refuse to acknowledge?

The ayah concludes powerfully: وَبِنِعْمَتِ ٱللَّهِ هُمْ يَكْفُرُونَ —"And in Allah's favor, these are the people who are in fact ungrateful." The word كُفْر here carries both meanings of denial and ingratitude—a complete rejection and refusal to acknowledge what is obvious.

translation: And they worship besides Allah that which does not possess for them [the power of] provision from the heavens and the earth at all, and [in fact], they are unable
commentary:
**وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَهُمْ رِزْقًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ**

This ayah exposes the absurdity of worshiping anything besides Allah ﷻ. The Quran says, "And they worship besides Allah what possesses no authority for them in terms of provision—neither from the heavens nor from the earth—anything at all, nor are they even capable of it."

The mention of "the heavens and the earth" is particularly significant because in polytheistic religions, different gods are assigned different domains and responsibilities. There's a god of the sky, a god of rain, a god of crops, a god of wealth—each deity responsible for a specific department of existence. It's almost like going to different government departments: you go to the water department for water services, the transportation department for street maintenance and so on.

In Hinduism today, for example, there's Lakshmi, the goddess of wealth, so people worship her when seeking a promotion. There are other deities for marriage, for health, for different needs. Allah ﷻ completely dismantles this concept by stating that these false objects of worship have no authority—لَا يَمْلِكُ —over anything in the heavens or the earth. They control nothing.

And it gets worse: وَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ —"nor are they even capable." They don't just lack authority over these things; they're not even capable of controlling these matters for themselves, let alone for you.

translation: So do not assert similarities to Allah. Indeed, Allah knows and you do not know
commentary:
**فَلَا تَضْرِبُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ**

Allah ﷻ commands: فَلَا تَضْرِبُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ —"Do not give examples about Allah." Only Allah ﷻ can do that. To Him alone belongs the highest example.

This is a critical theological point. While nothing can be compared to Allah ﷻ in His essence—لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَىْءٌ —Allah ﷻ Himself may give examples to teach us something about His relationship with creation. Notice the distinction: Allah ﷻ never gives examples about His attributes, His names or His being. What He does illustrate through examples are the relationships—like the relationship between a master and a slave, or between a provider and the one receiving provision.

This prohibition against giving examples about Allah ﷻ strikes at the heart of theological corruption in false religions. Consider what some Christians say when trying to explain the Trinity: "God is like a chair with multiple legs but still one chair," or "like an egg with shell, white and yolk but still one egg," or "like water that can be steam, ice or liquid but still H₂O." These are all attempts to give examples about Allah's ﷻ essence, which He has forbidden.

The modern atheist falls into the same trap. They ask, "Why does God want us to worship Him? Why does He want our thanks? Anyone who wants constant praise is arrogant and full of themselves!" They're taking human characteristics—where indeed, a person who constantly demands praise is arrogant—and projecting them onto their understanding of Allah ﷻ. This is exactly the problem: giving human examples to try to understand the Divine.

Allah ﷻ concludes: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ —"Indeed, Allah knows, and you do not know." He knows how to give proper examples; we don't.

translation: Allah presents an example: a slave [who is] owned and unable to do a thing and he to whom We have provided from Us good provision, so he spends from it secretly and publicly. Can they be equal? Praise to Allah! But most of them do not know
commentary:
**ضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا عَبْدًا مَّمْلُوكًا لَّا يَقْدِرُ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ وَمَن رَّزَقْنَـٰهُ مِنَّا رِزْقًا حَسَنًا فَهُوَ يُنفِقُ مِنْهُ سِرًّا وَجَهْرًا ۖ هَلْ يَسْتَوُۥنَ ۚ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ**

Immediately after saying "don't give examples," Allah ﷻ gives an example Himself—because He can, and because we need it to understand.

ضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا عَبْدًا مَّمْلُوكًا —"Allah gives the example of a slave who is owned." The language here is emphatic. A slave by definition is owned, but the phrase عَبْدًا مَّمْلُوكًا emphasizes complete ownership, absolute control. This slave has no free will whatsoever. He cannot choose what he eats, how many bites of food he takes, when he drinks water—he has no control over anything. لَّا يَقْدِرُ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ —"he has no capability over anything."

Now Allah ﷻ compares this completely restricted slave to another person: وَمَن رَّزَقْنَـٰهُ مِنَّا رِزْقًا حَسَنًا —"and the one whom We have provided from Us with good provision." Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't even call the second person a slave initially, though the context makes clear that both are servants of Allah ﷻ. The second one is described by what he's been given: special provision directly from Allah ﷻ, and not just any provision, but رِزْقًا حَسَنًا —excellent, beautiful provision.

Both are slaves, but one is completely restricted while the other appears wealthy and free. The lack of wealth can make you مَّمْلُوكًا —completely restricted. You might have elaborate ideas and plans, but without financial means, you cannot execute them. But this second slave's hands are open.

What does he do with this freedom and wealth? فَهُوَ يُنفِقُ مِنْهُ سِرًّا وَجَهْرًا —"He spends from it secretly and openly." The implication is that he spends in the path of Allah ﷻ. He has been given capability, and instead of serving only himself, he serves Allah ﷻ with what he's been given.

Why is spending specifically mentioned? Why not prayer or fasting or any other act of worship? Because the subject matter of this passage is provision—رِزْق. He was given excellent rizq, so he spends it. And he spends on every occasion he finds—secretly when appropriate, openly when needed.

Then comes the powerful question: هَلْ يَسْتَوُۥنَ —"Are they the same?" Can these two be counted as equal?

Here's the profound realization: you have been given an opportunity to earn great honor that other creations of Allah ﷻ don't have. All other creatures are like the first slave—مَّمْلُوكًا، completely under Allah's ﷻ authority with no choice. The bee doesn't choose whether to obey. The cow doesn't have the option to spend in Allah's ﷻ path. You, however, have been honored with choice. This is why Allah ﷻ said about the human being, لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ فِىٓ أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ (We have certainly created the human being in the best possible form). Why? Because he has choice, the ability to decide.

You're being put in a position of tremendous honor. When someone realizes they've been given such honor, what should naturally come out of their mouth? ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ —"All praise belongs to Allah." It should flow naturally, without needing elaborate explanation. You see the blessing, you say Alhamdulillah.

But the ayah concludes with a sobering reality: بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ —"But most of them don't know." They don't realize this. They don't understand what they've been given, so the gratitude doesn't come.

translation: And Allah presents an example of two men, one of them dumb and unable to do a thing, while he is a burden to his guardian. Wherever he directs him, he brings no good. Is he equal to one who commands justice, while he is on a straight path
commentary:
**وَضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا رَّجُلَيْنِ أَحَدُهُمَآ أَبْكَمُ لَا يَقْدِرُ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ وَهُوَ كَلٌّ عَلَىٰ مَوْلَىٰهُ أَيْنَمَا يُوَجِّههُّ لَا يَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ ۖ هَلْ يَسْتَوِى هُوَ وَمَن يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۙ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ**

Allah ﷻ gives another example: وَضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا رَّجُلَيْنِ —"And Allah gives the example of two men."

أَحَدُهُمَآ أَبْكَمُ —"One of them is mute." The word أَبْكَم means someone who cannot speak at all. لَا يَقْدِرُ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ —the description is repeated: "he is incapable of anything."

وَهُوَ كَلٌّ عَلَىٰ مَوْلَىٰهُ —"And he is a burden on his master." Imagine a master in those times who has slaves working for his business. They go out to make transactions, negotiate prices, ensure quality goods. But this slave is أَبْكَم—he cannot speak. When sent to make a sale or negotiate, he cannot communicate prices clearly, cannot get his point across, cannot negotiate effectively. He messes everything up. Yet the master still has to provide for him—feed him, house him, use resources on him. He's a complete burden, producing no benefit.

أَيْنَمَا يُوَجِّههُّ لَا يَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ —"Wherever he directs him, he brings no good." The master tries to turn him to different tasks, hoping to find something useful this slave can do, but nothing works. No matter where the master directs him, no good comes from it.

Now, anyone listening to this example is thinking about that useless slave. When the rhetorical question comes—هَلْ يَسْتَوِى هُوَ —"Is he the same...?"—you're expecting the comparison to be with a productive slave. Someone well-spoken, someone who brings good sales, someone who can represent the master well. Like Yusuf (AS) in the Biblical narrative, where the master (Al-Aziz) trusted him so completely that he handed over the keys to everything and only asked to be informed when dinner was ready.

But Allah ﷻ makes a brilliant shift. Instead of continuing with "is he the same as my useful slave," He says: وَمَن يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْعَدْلِ —"and the one who commands to justice."

This is a powerful transition. A typical slave owner doesn't expect his slave to go out and command people to justice. But Allah ﷻ expects this from His slaves. And notice what particular disability the useless slave had: he was أَبْكَم—unable to speak. Commanding to justice requires that you speak up. You must use your voice.

Allah ﷻ is highlighting a hidden reality among the Quraysh. This message of Islam made sense to them. When Muhammad ﷺ spoke about baby girls being buried alive, they agreed it was wrong. When he talked about orphans not being pushed around, they agreed. When he addressed people cheating in business, they agreed. Deep down, they knew he was right. But they remained أَبْكَم—mute. They couldn't say anything because speaking up would get them in trouble with the powerful elite of Makkah.

Allah ﷻ describes such a person as كَلٌّ—a burden on the earth. You are a waste of flesh, just a mouth that exists to eat, doing no good. The mouth that has worth to the Master is the one that يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْعَدْلِ—commands to justice.

وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ —"And he is upon a straight path." Why mention the straight path right after commanding to justice? Because when you speak up for what's right, you make enemies. When you make enemies, pressure mounts on you to change course, to compromise, to stay silent. But the one who truly commands to justice stays firm on the straight path, no matter the opposition.

translation: And to Allah belongs the unseen [aspects] of the heavens and the earth. And the command for the Hour is not but as a glance of the eye or even nearer. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
**وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَمَآ أَمْرُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ إِلَّا كَلَمْحِ ٱلْبَصَرِ أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ**

وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ —"And to Allah alone belongs the unseen of the heavens and the earth." Everything hidden, everything beyond our perception, belongs entirely to His knowledge and control.

وَمَآ أَمْرُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ إِلَّا كَلَمْحِ ٱلْبَصَرِ —"And the matter of the Hour is not except like the blink of an eye." The word لَمْح means a very brief glance or the blinking of an eye. The Day of Judgment will come that quickly—in a literal instant.

أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ —"Or it is even closer." Even faster than a blink.

This ayah connects powerfully to what came before. The previous ayahs discussed commanding to justice—عَدْل. Allah ﷻ is now saying: you're not establishing justice? Let Me tell you—I will establish justice. And My justice is very near, and I am completely capable of executing it.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ —"Indeed, Allah has complete control over all things." He has the power to bring about that Day of Justice whenever He wills, in the blink of an eye.

translation: And Allah has extracted you from the wombs of your mothers not knowing a thing, and He made for you hearing and vision and intellect that perhaps you would be grateful
commentary:
**وَٱللَّهُ أَخْرَجَكُم مِّنۢ بُطُونِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةَ ۙ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ**

Allah ﷻ says: وَٱللَّهُ أَخْرَجَكُم مِّنۢ بُطُونِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ —"And Allah brought you out from the bellies of your mothers."

Notice the connection to earlier imagery. This Surah previously mentioned بُطُون —bellies—in reference to cattle and what comes from their bellies. Now we're reminded of our own emergence from our mothers' bellies. But there's something deeper: when does the Quran mention you knowing nothing? لَا تَعْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا —"You knew nothing." This is mentioned at birth, and it's also mentioned at old age when you return to a state of not knowing, as referenced elsewhere in the Quran. Allah ﷻ is alluding to the cycle—you came from one form, you'll return to a similar state.

The previous ayah spoke about what event? يَوْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ —the Day of Judgment. You've already experienced coming from one stage of life into another. When you came out of your mother's belly, who brought you out? Allah ﷻ brought you out—أَخْرَجَكُم. Your mother didn't bring you out; Allah ﷻ did. And just as He brought you out that time, He will bring you out another time from another belly—the belly of the earth. That's why later in the Quran, the imagery appears: وَأَخْرَجَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ أَثْقَالَهَا —"the earth will bring out its burdens." The word أَثْقَال literally means the burdens of her womb. The earth will deliver one day, and Allah ﷻ will make it deliver.

وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةَ —"And He installed for you hearing, and sight, and hearts." These are the tools He gave you—the ability to hear, to see and to understand with your hearts and minds.

لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ —"So that you may be grateful." This is the ultimate purpose. The entire climate of these ayahs has been about creation and paying attention to it. The more you pay attention to Allah's ﷻ creation, the more it should make you grateful. You were brought up this way—programmed, designed—so you can reach the ultimate goal of being grateful. So you can reach Alhamdulillah.

translation: Do they not see the birds controlled in the atmosphere of the sky? None holds them up except Allah. Indeed in that are signs for a people who believe
commentary:
أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّيْرِ مُسَخَّرَٰتٍ فِى جَوِّ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَا يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ

Haven't they looked toward the birds? This ayah marks a subtle but significant shift in how Allah ﷻ addresses human awareness. In the previous passage, He mentioned the capabilities of hearing, seeing and the heart. Interestingly, Allah ﷻ skips over hearing in this ayah because the focus has been on listening to the message of revelation. Now the emphasis shifts to observation. The unspoken challenge is: You're not paying attention to this message, you're not listening to what you're supposed to. Okay, at least use your eyes.

The birds are described as مُسَخَّرَٰت - subdued, held in place. The root word taskheer means to overpower something and keep it in place, like pinning someone down. Animals that do what you tell them to do are described as musakhkhar. These birds exist فِى جَوِّ ٱلسَّمَآءِ - in the intense climate of the sky. The word jaww typically refers to something covered or an intense situation. Consider how winds can be intense - a bird caught in powerful winds might struggle to fly forward, hovering in the same place as if pinned mid-air.

مَا يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ - Nobody is holding them in place except Allah ﷻ. Then Allah ﷻ adds: إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ - certainly in that there are signs, using the plural form. The observation of a single bird suspended in air contains not one but multiple lessons. And who are these signs for? لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ - for people who seek to believe, who want to strengthen their iman.

What can we learn from a bird held suspended in the air? The wind represents difficulty for the bird, yet Allah ﷻ is holding it in the air through that very wind. In other words, Allah ﷻ is allowing what we perceive as difficulty to become the actual means of the bird's flight. When we face challenges in our lives, we should recognize that it is through these very challenges that we achieve height. It's through obstacles that we grow stronger in faith.

This concept inspired the famous Urdu poet Iqbal, who wrote addressing the Muslim youth: "The wind blows to make you fly higher - that's its purpose." The message is clear: don't be afraid when strong winds come your way. But who holds you in place during those winds? It's not your own strength - it's Allah ﷻ. That's what you must trust in. مَا يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ.

translation: And Allah has made for you from your homes a place of rest and made for you from the hides of the animals tents which you find light on your day of travel and your day of encampment; and from their wool, fur and hair is furnishing and enjoyment for a time
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنۢ بُيُوتِكُمْ سَكَنًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّن جُلُودِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ بُيُوتًا تَسْتَخِفُّونَهَا يَوْمَ ظَعْنِكُمْ وَيَوْمَ إِقَامَتِكُمْ ۙ وَمِنْ أَصْوَافِهَا وَأَوْبَارِهَا وَأَشْعَارِهَآ أَثَـٰثًا وَمَتَـٰعًا إِلَىٰ حِينٍ

Allah ﷻ made for you in your homes سَكَنًا - a place where you can find tranquility. This is significant because tranquility was already established inside the mother's womb, then you came out and Allah ﷻ gave you tranquility again in your homes. Notice that homes have already been mentioned in this surah - specifically when Allah ﷻ told the bees to build their homes. Just as He gave the bee its dwelling, He has given you a place to settle.

The ayah continues: مِّن جُلُودِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ بُيُوتًا - from the skins of cattle, He made homes for you. The Arabs would make tents from camel skin, cow skin and other animal hides, stretching them to create portable shelters. This was essential for their nomadic lifestyle.

تَسْتَخِفُّونَهَا - you try to keep your loads light. The word istikhaaf means to keep things light, especially when traveling. Think about how even today, when gas prices are high, we try to pack light because the heavier the car, the more fuel it consumes. In those times, this consideration was even more critical. You're loading goods onto a camel, and the distance it can travel depends on how exhausted it becomes, so keeping the burden light was essential. Interestingly, istikhaaf has another meaning beyond physical weight - it can also mean to keep a people weak socially, to diminish their influence and weight in society.

يَوْمَ ظَعْنِكُمْ - on the day of your travel. Dha'n is a specific type of travel where you're regularly on the move, making many stops, going from place to place repeatedly. This perfectly describes Bedouin life and their constant migration patterns.

وَمِنْ أَصْوَافِهَا وَأَوْبَارِهَا وَأَشْعَارِهَآ - and from their wool, their fur, and their hair. Soof specifically refers to the wool of sheep and goats. Awbar refers to the hair of camels. Sha'r is a general term for all animal hair, and interestingly, it can even include human hair, though here it refers to animal hair broadly.

أَثَـٰثًا وَمَتَـٰعًا إِلَىٰ حِينٍ - furniture and utility items for a time. In classical Arabic, athath encompasses all household items: furniture, rugs, curtains, utensils, and even small trivial items. Historically, it could even refer to slaves kept in the household - anything utilized in the home fell under this term. Mata' refers to means of enjoyment and utility, things you put to use until their appointed time.

translation: And Allah has made for you, from that which He has created, shadows and has made for you from the mountains, shelters and has made for you garments which protect you from the heat and garments which protect you from your [enemy in] battle. Thus does He complete His favor upon you that you might submit [to Him]
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّمَّا خَلَقَ ظِلَـٰلًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ أَكْنَـٰنًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ سَرَٰبِيلَ تَقِيكُمُ ٱلْحَرَّ وَسَرَٰبِيلَ تَقِيكُم بَأْسَكُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْلِمُونَ

Allah ﷻ placed for you from whatever He created ظِلَـٰلًا - shadows. These shadows are sometimes lifesavers, especially in hot climates. Then He mentions: مِّنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ أَكْنَـٰنًا - from the mountains, shelters. Aknan is the plural of kinn or kinaan. The root word kanana means to keep something secret, to hide it deep inside, to keep something out of harm's way. In desert climates, the biggest harm is the scorching sun. So aknan refers to caves in mountains that provide shelter from the climate. If you're climbing and caught in a storm or intense weather, you can hide inside the cave and wait until conditions improve.

سَرَٰبِيلَ appeared earlier at the end of Surah Ibrahim. Sarabeel refers to clothing - shirts, pants, your complete outfit. Allah ﷻ says He made outfits تَقِيكُمُ ٱلْحَرَّ - that protect you from heat. Then وَسَرَٰبِيلَ تَقِيكُم بَأْسَكُمْ - and outfits that protect you from warfare, meaning armor.

It's significant that Allah ﷻ says He "made" these things for you. Obviously, the blacksmith forges the armor and the tailor sews the clothing, but Allah ﷻ is declaring that ultimately, He made these for you. We saw the most beautiful expression of this in Surah Al-A'raf where Allah ﷻ said: "We sent down clothing upon you" - using the same language used for rain and revelation. Clothing is presented as a revealed gift from above.

كَذَٰلِكَ يُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ - that is how He completes His favor upon you. لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْلِمُونَ - so that you may be in submission to Him.

translation: But if they turn away, [O Muhammad] - then only upon you is [responsibility for] clear notification
commentary:
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ

فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ - then if they turn away. After all these signs, all these favors enumerated, if they still turn away, what is the Prophet's ﷺ responsibility? فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ - your only obligation is to deliver the message clearly. Clear communication, plain conveyance - that's all that's required of the Messenger ﷺ.

translation: They recognize the favor of Allah; then they deny it. And most of them are disbelievers
commentary:
يَعْرِفُونَ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُنكِرُونَهَا وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ

يَعْرِفُونَ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ - they recognize Allah's favor. In the previous ayahs, we learned about all these blessings. If they're denying them, perhaps the Prophet ﷺ might think it's because they simply don't know. But Allah ﷻ corrects this assumption: No, they do know the favor of Allah. ثُمَّ يُنكِرُونَهَا - then they deny it. They know it's there, they recognize it, and then they pretend it's not there. They consciously reject what they know to be true.

وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ - and most of them are the ultimate disbelievers. Why doesn't Allah ﷻ just say kafiroon? Why use the definite article al-kafiroon? What's special about these people? These individuals heard the Quran directly from the mouth of the Prophet ﷺ himself. They witnessed his character firsthand. And their very survival in Makkah's harsh climate is impossible without these miraculous favors of Allah ﷻ coming their way continuously. That's an extremely challenging environment for human beings to survive in. Despite all of this - the direct message, the living example, the constant evidence of divine provision - they still disbelieve. These are the ultimate disbelievers.

This is something we must truly appreciate about prophetic work. When we discuss disbelievers or non-Muslims today, their situation is nothing compared to the kuffar of Quraysh. Allah ﷻ sends His messengers to the toughest audiences, and He sent His final Messenger ﷺ to the absolute toughest audience imaginable. Not only do we have the best of the best among the Sahaba (RA), but we also witnessed the worst of the worst among the Quraysh. We will never find worse disbelievers than them. Nobody will ever see the Prophet ﷺ face-to-face and reject him again - that opportunity is gone. Nobody will ever encounter this message in a better way than they did: the best message ever revealed, the final revelation, delivered in their own language, in their own context, from the mouth of the ultimate teacher ever sent to humanity. And yet they still disbelieved. Everything that comes after them is mild compared to what they represented.

translation: And [mention] the Day when We will resurrect from every nation a witness. Then it will not be permitted to the disbelievers [to apologize or make excuses], nor will they be asked to appease [Allah]
commentary:
وَيَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيدًا ثُمَّ لَا يُؤْذَنُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَلَا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ

Look at the sequencing of these ayahs. First, Allah ﷻ tells the Messenger ﷺ: Your only obligation is to communicate. Then He says: They know the truth and deny it anyway - they're a hopeless case. Now He says: I will make you a witness against them, and then the trial will take place.

وَيَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيدًا - on the Day when We resurrect from every nation a witness. ثُمَّ لَا يُؤْذَنُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ - then those who disbelieved will not be given permission. One meaning: they won't be given an ear, their case won't be heard. There's a powerful connection here to something mentioned earlier in the surah about a slave who doesn't perform his duties - he is described as unable to speak. That imagery continues here. They cannot speak.

The other meaning of yu'dhan: they won't be given permission to speak at all. Either way, the result is the same - they are rendered speechless.

وَلَا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ - and they will not be allowed to make amends. This is a new word for us. 'Ataba means to reprimand someone, to scold them, to be harsh in your advice for their own benefit. Ista'taba is the opposite - it means trying to make someone happy with you again after they've been harsh toward you. When someone is upset with you and you go to them saying, "Come on, I didn't mean it, I'm sorry, please" - that's ista'tab.

People do this all the time in their relationships. Someone snaps at their spouse, says something harsh, then feels bad and comes back trying to smooth things over: "I didn't mean it, sorry, I was just watching the game and got carried away." That's ista'tab. On the Day of Judgment, these same people who were so arrogant will try to come before Allah ﷻ and do ista'tab - "I'm so sorry, please, give me another chance." But they won't even be given that opportunity.

Think of a student who fails an exam, then comes to the teacher: "Please, I'll do better, I promise. I'll come early to class from now on, I won't even go home, I'll live in the classroom!" That's ista'tab. وَلَا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ - they will not be given a chance to make these pleas or amends.

translation: And when those who wronged see the punishment, it will not be lightened for them, nor will they be reprieved
commentary:
وَإِذَا رَءَا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ فَلَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ

وَإِذَا رَءَا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ - and when those who did wrong see the punishment. فَلَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمْ - it will not be lightened from them, not reduced in the slightest.

وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ - and they won't even be looked at. Think about what criminals do when they're in a cell and the warden walks by, or a lawyer passes - they try to get attention: "Hey, can I get your attention? Just look at me, please look at me!" But these people won't even receive a glance. Nobody will look their way.

translation: And when those who associated others with Allah see their "partners," they will say," Our Lord, these are our partners [to You] whom we used to invoke besides You." But they will throw at them the statement, "Indeed, you are liars
commentary:
وَإِذَا رَءَا ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ شُرَكَآءَهُمْ قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ شُرَكَآؤُنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كُنَّا نَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِكَ ۖ فَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلَ إِنَّكُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ

وَإِذَا رَءَا ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ شُرَكَآءَهُمْ - and when those who committed shirk see their associates. These are the idols they worshipped, the prophets they elevated beyond their station, the scholars they followed blindly, the righteous people whose graves they prostrated to. They're going to see these figures on Judgment Day.

Picture the scene: they see these scholars and saints walking by. "Hey! Abdul Qadir Jilani! I love you! I did so many prostrations at your grave! Can you help us now?" They're calling out desperately. قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ شُرَكَآؤُنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كُنَّا نَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِكَ - they'll say, "Our Master, these are our associates, the ones we used to call upon besides You!" They're hoping these figures will intercede, will help them out.

But here's the critical point: most of these people they're calling to were actually righteous individuals. Did these righteous people have any idea this was happening with their names or at their graves? Absolutely not. This is the first time they're learning about it.

فَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلَ إِنَّكُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ - so they throw the word back at them: "You've got to be kidding me. No way. You people are liars!" They're shocked, bewildered. They have no idea what these people are talking about. They walk away saying, "I don't know anything about this. You used to call on me besides Allah? I'm heading toward Allah myself - you can go where you're going."

The word kadhiboon (liars) is significant here. These followers might claim that these righteous people themselves promoted such practices, but they didn't. The righteous people want nothing to do with this association. They refuse to be linked to these practices at all.

translation: And they will impart to Allah that Day [their] submission, and lost from them is what they used to invent
commentary:
وَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلسَّلَمَ ۖ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ

When that hope is gone - when the intermediaries they counted on have disowned them - who must they deal with directly now? Allah ﷻ. وَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلسَّلَمَ - then they throw submission before Allah. The expression "to throw submission" means to give yourself up completely, to throw yourself before someone, to grovel at their feet. That's the figurative meaning in Arabic, though it doesn't translate as naturally into English.

They come before Allah ﷻ groveling, crying, throwing themselves in complete submission. وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ - and everything they used to fabricate has been lost from them. They can't find any of it anymore. All those invented beliefs, those false hopes, those intermediaries they relied on - all of it has vanished.

translation: Those who disbelieved and averted [others] from the way of Allah - We will increase them in punishment over [their] punishment for what corruption they were causing
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ زِدْنَـٰهُمْ عَذَابًا فَوْقَ ٱلْعَذَابِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يُفْسِدُونَ

ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - those who disbelieved and obstructed from Allah's path. Why will they receive double punishment?

First, being a disbeliever is already severe enough. You're already in serious trouble on Judgment Day just for your own disbelief. But what did these people do in addition? وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - they obstructed the path of Allah. They didn't just stop themselves - they became active enemies of the truth. They blocked others from finding guidance. They opposed this mission, this cause, this truth. On top of being enemies to themselves through their own disbelief, they became enemies to Allah's message and prevented others from accepting it.

زِدْنَـٰهُمْ عَذَابًا فَوْقَ ٱلْعَذَابِ - We have increased them in punishment above and beyond the punishment already there. An awesome, overwhelming punishment layered upon the punishment they already deserve for their disbelief.

بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يُفْسِدُونَ - on account of the corruption they created. Notice the verb form: yufsidoon means they were actively creating corruption, spreading filth and destruction. It's not just that they were corrupt themselves - if your translation only says "because they were corrupt," that translation is incomplete and doesn't capture the full meaning. The correct understanding is: on account of the corruption they actively created and spread in the world. This is the true crime that warrants the multiplied punishment.

translation: And [mention] the Day when We will resurrect among every nation a witness over them from themselves. And We will bring you, [O Muhammad], as a witness over your nation. And We have sent down to you the Book as clarification for all things and as guidance and mercy and good tidings for the Muslims
commentary:
وَيَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ فِى كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيدًا عَلَيْهِم مِّنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ وَجِئْنَا بِكَ شَهِيدًا عَلَىٰ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ۚ وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ تِبْيَـٰنًا لِّكُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ

This ayah establishes the concept of divine testimony on the Day of Judgment. Allah ﷻ says: "On the day when We will resurrect in every nation a witness against them from within themselves, and We will bring you as a witness against these people."

This is the second time we encounter this concept in the Quran. The first occurrence appears in Surah An-Nisa, but with a different sequence and emphasis. That surah is Madani, revealed to the Muslim community in Madinah, while Surah An-Nahl is Makki, revealed to a different audience in Makkah. Understanding this distinction helps us appreciate the different rhetorical approaches Allah ﷻ uses.

In An-Nisa, the ayah addresses the believers directly, creating a sense of accountability even for Muslims themselves—that the Prophet ﷺ will stand as a witness over them regarding whether they fulfilled their responsibilities. This creates a moment of reflection and even shock for the believers: even we will be held accountable through this testimony.

Here in An-Nahl, the sequencing doesn't create that same sense of surprise because the natural role of a messenger is to be a witness against those who disbelieved in him. The statement flows logically: every nation had a messenger, and those messengers will testify.

The ayah continues: "And We have sent down upon you the Book as a clarification for everything." The word تِبْيَانًا is notable—it's a rare grammatical form, an extremely elaborate way of expressing clarification. This isn't just explanation; it's comprehensive, detailed exposition of everything (لِّكُلِّ شَىْءٍ).

The Quran is then described with three more qualities: guidance (هُدًى), mercy (رَحْمَةً) and good news (بُشْرَىٰ) specifically for those who submit themselves (لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ). Each of these terms deserves deep reflection, and we'll explore some manifestations of تِبْيَان throughout our discussion of these ayahs.

translation: Indeed, Allah orders justice and good conduct and giving to relatives and forbids immorality and bad conduct and oppression. He admonishes you that perhaps you will be reminded
commentary:
۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْعَدْلِ وَٱلْإِحْسَـٰنِ وَإِيتَآئِ ذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَيَنْهَىٰ عَنِ ٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَٱلْمُنكَرِ وَٱلْبَغْىِ ۚ يَعِظُكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ

This is one of the most frequently quoted ayahs in Friday sermons. Allah ﷻ says: "Indeed, Allah commands justice (الْعَدْلِ), excellence (الْإِحْسَانِ), and giving to relatives, and He forbids immorality (الْفَحْشَاءِ), wrongdoing (الْمُنكَرِ), and oppression (الْبَغْيِ). He advises you so that perhaps you will remember."

Let's begin with understanding the relationship between justice and excellence. Justice represents the bare minimum expected from a decent human being—not even specifically a Muslim, but any decent person. When someone lives by justice alone, this isn't an accomplishment; it simply means they're not a criminal. Justice is the starting point, the minimum expectation Allah ﷻ has from humanity.

But Allah ﷻ doesn't want us to stop at justice. He commands us to reach beyond it to إِحْسَان —excellence, beauty, perfection in our actions. The entire spectrum of good deeds is captured between these two poles: the lowest you should reach is justice, and the highest you should strive for is excellence.

Then Allah ﷻ mentions a specific application: giving to those who possess closeness (ذِي الْقُرْبَىٰ), meaning close relatives. This might seem like just another example, but there's profound wisdom in why Allah ﷻ highlights this particular obligation. When we think of justice, we typically imagine the highest levels of society—courts, legal systems, governments. But Islam's concept of justice, though it may sound rhetorical or cliche, is actually founded on realistic wisdom: a society standing on justice cannot be built unless families have justice within them.

You will never have a society of justice until you have families of justice. You will not have a society of excellence until you have families of excellence. We must start practicing justice within our families, because at the end of the day, all society is composed of families, and those families are composed of individuals. It starts at the individual level, ripples out to the family, and then extends beyond.

After highlighting the family foundation, Allah ﷻ identifies what threatens it. The word الْفَحْشَاء refers to shamelessness, lewdness, obscenity, and vulgarity. This is the enemy of family structure. On one side, you have nikah (marriage), you have order, hierarchy, the roles of men and women, responsibilities toward children—the entire structure Allah ﷻ ordained for human beings to live in families. On the opposite end, you have the chaotic force that destroys family structure: fahsha.

There's an important distinction made in this ayah between الْفَحْشَاء and الْمُنكَر. Munkar is that which is recognized as evil—clearly wrong actions like lying, murder, and stealing that nobody has confusion about. These are obviously bad, the opposite of ma'ruf (what is known to be good).

Fahsha, however, includes things people might be tempted by or try to justify. It encompasses obscene speech, obscene art, obscene entertainment, obscene company, obscene behavior, and obscene businesses. Someone might say, "Oh no, this is just how we do things," and attempt to normalize what is actually shameful. Fahsha creates doors to evil, opens pathways to corruption that affect entire communities.

The ayah concludes with الْبَغْي —rebellion and oppression. This typically refers to religious forms of rebellion, transgressing the boundaries Allah ﷻ has set.

Allah ﷻ says, "He advises you (يَعِظُكُمْ) so that perhaps you will remember (تَذَكَّرُونَ)." This isn't just information; it's counsel meant to awaken our consciousness and bring us back to what we already know in our fitrah.

translation: And fulfill the covenant of Allah when you have taken it, [O believers], and do not break oaths after their confirmation while you have made Allah, over you, a witness. Indeed, Allah knows what you do
commentary:
وَأَوْفُوا۟ بِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ إِذَا عَـٰهَدتُّمْ وَلَا تَنقُضُوا۟ ٱلْأَيْمَـٰنَ بَعْدَ تَوْكِيدِهَا وَقَدْ جَعَلْتُمُ ٱللَّهَ عَلَيْكُمْ كَفِيلًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا تَفْعَلُونَ

Starting with this ayah, there's an interesting scholarly discussion about who is being addressed. Some scholars say these ayahs speak to all human beings, referring to the primordial covenant: "Am I not your Lord?" (أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ) that all souls made with Allah ﷻ before creation.

However, another view—which I incline toward—is that this specifically addresses the People of the Book, particularly the Jewish community of Madinah. As we continue through these ayahs, you'll notice increasing hints pointing toward this audience.

"Fulfill the covenant with Allah when you have made a covenant." The Jews already had confirmation through their scholarly texts that Muhammad ﷺ was indeed a prophet. They possessed knowledge passed down through their tradition that verified his coming.

"And do not violate your oaths after you have confirmed them." The phrase بَعْدَ تَوْكِيدِهَا (after affirming them) is significant. After that knowledge was confirmed, after those oaths were made, don't now cancel out your iman, your belief.

"And you had already made Allah the Overseer (كَفِيلًا) over yourselves." Historical accounts tell us that the Jews of Madinah used to fight against the Quraysh and other Arab tribes. When they would lose battles, they would say, "Our final prophet is coming, and when he comes, you're going to get the beating of your life. God is witness." They invoked Allah ﷻ as witness to their belief in the coming prophet. Now that he has arrived, they're forgetting all these affirmations they made.

The ayah concludes: "Indeed, Allah knows what you are doing (مَا تَفْعَلُونَ)." Nothing is hidden from Him.

translation: And do not be like she who untwisted her spun thread after it was strong [by] taking your oaths as [means of] deceit between you because one community is more plentiful [in number or wealth] than another community. Allah only tries you thereby. And He will surely make clear to you on the Day of Resurrection that over which you used to differ
commentary:
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّتِى نَقَضَتْ غَزْلَهَا مِنۢ بَعْدِ قُوَّةٍ أَنكَـٰثًا تَتَّخِذُونَ أَيْمَـٰنَكُمْ دَخَلًۢا بَيْنَكُمْ أَن تَكُونَ أُمَّةٌ هِىَ أَرْبَىٰ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ ۚ إِنَّمَا يَبْلُوكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦ ۚ وَلَيُبَيِّنَنَّ لَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ

Allah ﷻ now provides a powerful metaphor: "Don't be like the woman who undid her weaving." The word نَقَضَتْ means to tear apart, and غَزْلَهَا refers to her weaving—her spun thread or woven cloth.

Imagine a woman who weaves for years, carefully working thread by thread, until she creates something strong (مِنْ بَعْدِ قُوَّةٍ —after it had become strong). Then she takes it and tears it into shreds (أَنكَاثًا —torn fragments). When you see someone doing this—weaving patiently and then suddenly ripping it all apart—you'd think she's lost her mind. What's wrong with her?

This is the imagery Allah ﷻ uses for those who have the entire text of the Torah before them, who have carefully studied every ayah, who have put all the evidence together and know with certainty that Muhammad ﷺ is the Messenger of Allah—and then proceed to undo it all. They reinterpret this verse, then that verse, then another, systematically tearing their own book apart just to ensure nobody believes in this prophet.

Why would they do this? "You have taken your oaths among yourselves as means of interference دَخَلًا. The word dakhalan refers to something that interferes, that gets in the way. Their concern was: "A nation should become more powerful أَرْبَىٰ than another nation."

The word أَرْبَىٰ comes from the root ر-ب-و, which literally means growth. They feared that if they accepted Muhammad ﷺ, the Arabs would grow more powerful, would get ahead, would supersede them. This became their internal conflict, the real reason behind their rejection—not lack of evidence, but fear of losing status.

"Allah is only testing you by means of it إِنَّمَا يَبْلُوكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِهِ." This situation itself is a test. Will you follow the truth regardless of worldly consequences, or will you let tribal loyalties and fear of losing power prevent you from accepting what you know is right?

"And He will absolutely clarify for you on the Day of Resurrection what you used to differ about." The phrase وَلَيُبَيِّنَنَّ includes emphatic particles—the lam of emphasis and the doubled nun. Allah ﷻ is swearing: He will definitely, certainly make clear on that Day all the matters you disputed, all the reinterpretations you made, all the truth you tried to obscure.

translation: And if Allah had willed, He could have made you [of] one religion, but He causes to stray whom He wills and guides whom He wills. And you will surely be questioned about what you used to do
commentary:
وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَلَـٰكِن يُضِلُّ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَلَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ عَمَّا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

"If Allah had willed, He would have made you one nation." This is a profound statement about divine wisdom and human will. Allah ﷻ could have created all of humanity upon one religion, with no diversity of belief, no test of faith. But He didn't.

"However, He lets go astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills." This phrase requires careful understanding. The structure وَلَـٰكِن يُضِلُّ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ presents both divine will and human will simultaneously.

From one perspective, مَن يَشَاءُ (whom He wills) refers to Allah's will: He guides and misguides according to His wisdom. From another perspective, it refers to human will: He misguides whoever wants misguidance and guides whoever wants guidance. Both readings are valid and both are true simultaneously. Allah's will and human will operate together—He guides those who seek guidance and allows those who choose misguidance to go astray.

This ayah has profound implications for how we understand our role in calling people to Islam. We must have complete confidence in these words: if we make the word of Allah ﷻ known, then whoever truly wants guidance will receive it. It is not our responsibility to carve out a path for them to Islam or to convert anybody. Our job is to clarify guidance, to teach it, to make it accessible.

Allah ﷻ Himself has said He will guide whoever He wants—whoever seeks it with sincerity. He's left that door open, and we cannot close it. We have no right to say, "These people will never believe," or to become pessimistic about the message reaching hearts. That's not the outlook we're allowed to have.

Some might wonder: "What about people who want guidance but get confused by different religions, different cultures, different misunderstandings?" Allah ﷻ addresses this concern. Those options will exist—various paths, various confusions—but if someone really wants truth, Allah ﷻ has taken the responsibility to guide them to Islam. That's not your responsibility.

"As for those who strive in Our cause, We will guide them to Our ways (وَالَّذِينَ جَاهَدُوا فِينَا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنَا)." When someone makes sincere effort, Allah ﷻ opens pathways. Think about the journeys converts to Islam describe—how many stops they had to make, how many turns in the road, how unexpected the path was. We cannot project a calculated map of "how we're going to bring Islam to America" or any other place. You clarify the Book—that's it. Then Allah ﷻ makes roads for those with sincerity, and you will not believe the roads He makes. The paths are sometimes strange, unexpected, miraculous.

I recall a brother in New York who played in the NFL back in the 1970s. His path to Islam began during a football game when, from a distance in the crowd, he saw someone praying in the bleachers. This image stayed with him. He kept wondering about it, asking around: "Was that a prayer? What kind of prayer?" This was the 1970s, when Islam wasn't widespread in America, so he had to search extensively before finding answers. Eventually, he found a copy of the Quran in a library, started reading, and that changed everything.

Years later, this same brother would walk to the masjid every day at three in the morning—a 20-minute walk—open the gates, make coffee, give the adhan, and maintain this practice with unwavering consistency. You'd never predict that someone's journey to Islam would begin by glimpsing someone praying during an NFL game. But Allah ﷻ guides whom He wills through means we could never calculate.

The ayah concludes: "And you will definitely be questioned about what you used to do (وَلَتُسْأَلُنَّ عَمَّا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ)." This is emphatic—the lam and doubled nun again. There's no escape from accountability. Every action, every choice, every moment of accepting or rejecting truth—all of it will be brought forth and questioned. 

This ending reminds us that while Allah ﷻ guides whom He wills and allows whom He wills to go astray, each person bears responsibility for their choices. The test is real, the questioning is certain, and the consequences are just.

translation: And do not take your oaths as [means of] deceit between you, lest a foot slip after it was [once] firm, and you would taste evil [in this world] for what [people] you diverted from the way of Allah, and you would have [in the Hereafter] a great punishment
commentary:
**وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَكُمْ دَخَلًۢا بَيْنَكُمْ فَتَزِلَّ قَدَمٌۢ بَعْدَ ثُبُوتِهَا وَتَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلسُّوٓءَ بِمَا صَدَدتُّمْ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَلَكُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ**

"Do not take your oaths as a means of interference among yourselves, lest your foot should slip after it was firmly established, and you taste evil because you obstructed from the path of Allah ﷻ, and for you is a tremendous punishment."

Allah ﷻ warns us against misusing our oaths as internal mechanisms within our communities. The imagery here is striking: فَتَزِلَّ قَدَمٌۢ بَعْدَ ثُبُوتِهَا—"lest your foot should slip after it was firmly established." The word قَدَم, which means foot, is grammatically feminine because body parts that come in pairs are treated as feminine in Arabic. This ayah paints a picture of someone who was standing firm and stable, but then loses their footing and falls.

The consequence of misusing oaths is severe: وَتَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلسُّوٓءَ—"you will taste evil." Why? بِمَا صَدَدتُّمْ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ—"because of the obstructions you made from the path of Allah ﷻ." When we manipulate our oaths or use them deceitfully, we don't just harm ourselves. We create barriers that prevent others from approaching the path of Allah ﷻ. We obstruct both ourselves and those around us from guidance. For those who engage in such behavior, the warning is clear: وَلَكُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ—"and for you especially is a tremendous punishment."

translation: And do not exchange the covenant of Allah for a small price. Indeed, what is with Allah is best for you, if only you could know
commentary:
**وَلَا تَشْتَرُوا۟ بِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۚ إِنَّمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ**

"And do not sell the promise made with Allah ﷻ for a minuscule gain. Indeed, what is with Allah ﷻ alone is better for you, if you only knew."

This ayah builds directly on the previous warning about oaths. Allah ﷻ commands us not to exchange His covenant for ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا—a small, insignificant price. The connection becomes clear: when people manipulate their oaths for worldly gain, they're essentially selling their commitment to Allah ﷻ for something trivial and temporary.

But then Allah ﷻ provides the antidote to this temptation: إِنَّمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ—"Indeed, what is with Allah ﷻ alone is better for you." Notice the emphasis here. The pronoun هُوَ adds emphasis: it is *only* what Allah ﷻ has that is truly better. Everything else is inferior. The ayah concludes with a gentle reminder: إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ—"if you only knew." If we truly understood and internalized this reality, we would never be tempted to trade the eternal for the temporary.

translation: Whatever you have will end, but what Allah has is lasting. And We will surely give those who were patient their reward according to the best of what they used to do
commentary:
**مَا عِندَكُمْ يَنفَدُ ۖ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ بَاقٍ ۗ وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوٓا۟ أَجْرَهُم بِأَحْسَنِ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ**

"What you have will run out, but what Allah ﷻ has is lasting. And We will absolutely compensate those who were patient with their reward according to the best of what they used to do."
