Allah ﷻ now presents a fundamental truth about the nature of worldly possessions versus divine rewards. The word يَنفَدُ comes from the root نَفَدَ, meaning to run out, to be exhausted, to cease to exist. Its opposite is بَقِيَ —to remain, to endure. Notice the linguistic beauty here: مَا عِندَكُمْ يَنفَدُ is expressed with a verb (yanfad), emphasizing the temporary, changing nature of worldly possessions. But مَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ بَاقٍ uses an ism, a noun (baqin), suggesting permanence and unchanging stability. What remains is permanent like a noun, and what dissipates is temporary like a verb.

Then comes an extraordinary promise: وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوٓا۟ أَجْرَهُم—"And We will absolutely compensate those who were patient with their reward." But compensation for what exactly? بِأَحْسَنِ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ—"according to the best of what they used to do."

This is remarkable. Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't say "We will give them jaza' (compensation) for their deeds." Instead, He says "We will give them their ajr (reward)." The shift from jaza' to ajr is significant. This reward will be calculated based on your *best* deeds, not your average performance. 

Think about what this means practically. All of us have varying quality in our worship. Some of our prayers are exceptional—tears flowing, full concentration, every ayah resonating in our hearts. Other prayers? We're not even sure if we prayed two or three rak'ahs. Sometimes our Quran recitation is beautiful and meaningful; other times we don't even know what just happened. But if we remain among those who practice sabr—consistency, perseverance, staying the course despite our ups and downs—Allah ﷻ will grade us on a curve. He takes our best deeds and pulls everything else up to that standard. Your best salah becomes the standard for all your prayers. Your best Quran recitation becomes the measure for all your recitations.

Why is this specifically for those who have sabr? Because sabr doesn't just mean patience in the sense of enduring difficulty. It means consistency and constancy. You stay at it. You're not seasonal in your commitment to Allah ﷻ. Yes, it's impossible for human beings to maintain absolute consistency, but as long as you keep trying, as long as you stay committed to the path, you will be rewarded according to the best of your work.

translation: Whoever does righteousness, whether male or female, while he is a believer - We will surely cause him to live a good life, and We will surely give them their reward [in the Hereafter] according to the best of what they used to do
commentary:
**مَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُۥ حَيَوٰةً طَيِّبَةً ۖ وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُم بِأَحْسَنِ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ**

"Whoever does righteous deeds, whether male or female, while being a believer—We will surely grant them a good life, and We will reward them according to the best of what they used to do."

The phrase مَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا can be understood in two ways. If we look at صَـٰلِحًا as a noun, it means "whoever did a single good deed." If we understand it as functioning adverbially, it means "whoever acted righteously" as a complete way of life. Both meanings are valid and beautiful.

But notice the critical qualifications: مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ—whether male or female. Gender creates no barrier to this promise. Then comes the essential condition: وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ—"while being a believer." Allah ﷻ qualifies righteous action with iman. You can't separate belief from action. There are people who don't believe yet do good deeds, but they don't qualify for this promise. When these two elements come together—righteous action and genuine faith—then the promise unfolds.

فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُۥ حَيَوٰةً طَيِّبَةً—"We will certainly give them a beautiful life." Not just any life, but a حَيَوٰةً طَيِّبَةً—a life described as good, pure and wholesome. This is the life in this world, a life characterized by contentment, peace and barakah. And beyond that: وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُم بِأَحْسَنِ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ—"And We will compensate them with their reward according to the best of what they used to do." The promise from the previous ayah is repeated here, cementing this beautiful principle of divine mercy.

translation: So when you recite the Qur'an, [first] seek refuge in Allah from Satan, the expelled [from His mercy]
commentary:
**فَإِذَا قَرَأْتَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ٱلرَّجِيمِ**

"So when you recite the Quran, seek refuge with Allah ﷻ from Satan, the accursed."

After discussing the importance of doing our best and remaining consistent in our deeds, Allah ﷻ now highlights a specific act of worship: recitation of the Quran. But why does this ayah appear here, right after the conversation about consistency and doing good deeds?

The answer lies in understanding what creates inconsistency in our worship, particularly in our relationship with the Quran. What prevents us from maintaining regular, meaningful recitation? What disrupts our consistency? Shaytan. Therefore, the remedy is given immediately: فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ٱلرَّجِيمِ—"seek refuge with Allah ﷻ from Satan, the accursed."

This isn't just a ritual statement we make before recitation. It's a profound recognition that our greatest obstacle to benefiting from the Quran, to maintaining consistency in our worship, is the whispers and attacks of Shaytan. By seeking Allah's protection first, we arm ourselves against the force that would otherwise prevent us from this great good deed.

translation: Indeed, there is for him no authority over those who have believed and rely upon their Lord
commentary:
**إِنَّهُۥ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ سُلْطَـٰنٌ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ**

"Indeed, he has no authority over those who believe and place their trust in their Lord."

Someone might ask: what does the pronoun إِنَّهُۥ (innahu) refer to? Who is "he"? The answer is that this is an emphatic exclamation—a statement of fact that demands our attention. Listen carefully: إِنَّهُۥ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ سُلْطَـٰنٌ—"he has absolutely no authority." 

Over whom does Shaytan lack authority? عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟—over those who believe. But there's an additional qualification: وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ—"and upon their Lord they place their trust." Notice that trust in Allah ﷻ is the highlight here, the defining characteristic that shields believers from Shaytan's influence.

Why is tawakkul (reliance on Allah ﷻ) so critical? Because when you don't rely on Allah ﷻ, that's when Shaytan finds his opening. He comes and plants thoughts: "Your life is terrible. Why didn't this work out? Why didn't that happen?" He fills you with regrets about the past and anxieties about the future: "What if this doesn't work out? What if that goes wrong?" A constant stream of worry, stress and doubt.

Consider this: most psychological struggles—stress, depression, anxiety—stem from either dwelling on past events or worrying about future possibilities. What's the remedy? Tawakkul. Whatever happened in the past, I trust Allah ﷻ. There was wisdom in it, whether I understand it fully or not. Whether it involved sickness, family troubles, death, financial loss—whatever it was, tawakkul is the answer. 

When tawakkul is lacking, Shaytan gains a foothold. He starts flooding your mind with questions, complaints and doubts. The cycle becomes relentless. You find yourself held hostage by waswasah (whispers) that won't stop. That's when you know something has gotten hold of you. The remedy? Return to the Quran. Return to seeking refuge with Allah ﷻ. The words of Allah ﷻ themselves become counsel, better than any human counselor could provide.

translation: His authority is only over those who take him as an ally and those who through him associate others with Allah
commentary:
**إِنَّمَا سُلْطَـٰنُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُۥ وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِهِۦ مُشْرِكُونَ**

"His authority is only over those who take him as an ally, and those who associate others with Allah ﷻ through him."

Now Allah ﷻ defines the boundaries of Shaytan's power. His authority—his sultan—extends only over two categories of people. First: ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُۥ—"those who take him as an ally," those who turn toward him, who befriend him and follow his suggestions. Second: وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِهِۦ مُشْرِكُونَ—"those who associate others with Allah ﷻ through him."

This second phrase can be understood in multiple ways. It could mean those who commit shirk while following Shaytan's path. Or it could mean those who associate partners with Allah ﷻ *by means of* Shaytan—meaning they allow Shaytan to be a partner in their decision-making, in their thoughts, in their lives, to the point where his whispers compete with divine guidance in their hearts.

The contrast with the previous ayah is stark. Those who have iman and tawakkul are protected. Those who abandon iman and tawakkul become subject to Shaytan's authority.

translation: And when We substitute a verse in place of a verse - and Allah is most knowing of what He sends down - they say, "You, [O Muhammad], are but an inventor [of lies]." But most of them do not know
commentary:
**وَإِذَا بَدَّلْنَآ ءَايَةً مَّكَانَ ءَايَةٍ ۙ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يُنَزِّلُ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُفْتَرٍۭ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ**

"And when We replace a miraculous sign in place of another sign—and Allah ﷻ knows best what He sends down—they say, 'You are just fabricating.' Rather, most of them do not know."

This ayah addresses an accusation leveled against the Prophet ﷺ. Some scholars interpret this as referring to naskh (abrogation)—when one legal ruling in the Quran replaces another. However, given that Surah An-Nahl is a Makkan surah revealed before most legal rulings were established, there's a strong case for an alternative interpretation.

The more likely meaning here relates to the similarity between various ayahs in the Quran. When the Prophet ﷺ would recite revelation, sometimes the ayahs would be very similar to each other but with subtle differences. One day he might recite an ayah that begins a certain way and ends a particular way. The next day, he recites another ayah that starts similarly but concludes differently. The disbelievers would hear this and accuse him: "Yesterday you said it one way, today you're saying it differently. You're making this up as you go!"

They failed to understand that these were different ayahs revealed for different contexts, not the same ayah being changed. When Allah ﷻ says وَإِذَا بَدَّلْنَآ ءَايَةً مَّكَانَ ءَايَةٍ—"when We replace an ayah in place of another ayah"—it means when one ayah comes in the discourse after another, not necessarily that it abrogates it legally.

Allah's response is decisive: وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يُنَزِّلُ—"Allah ﷻ knows best what He sends down." Only He knows the wisdom behind which revelation comes when and why certain ayahs bear similarities to others. But the accusers قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُفْتَرٍۭ—they say, "You're nothing but a fabricator." 

The response? بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ—"Rather, most of them have no knowledge." Their accusation stems from ignorance, not understanding. They can't conceive of the sophistication of divine revelation, so they resort to the simplest explanation their limited understanding can produce: he must be making it up.

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "The Pure Spirit has brought it down from your Lord in truth to make firm those who believe and as guidance and good tidings to the Muslims
commentary:
**قُلْ نَزَّلَهُۥ رُوحُ ٱلْقُدُسِ مِن رَّبِّكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِيُثَبِّتَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ**

"Say: The Holy Spirit has sent it down from your Lord with truth, so that He may strengthen those who believe, and as guidance and good news for those who submit."

Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ to respond to the accusation. قُلْ—"Say": نَزَّلَهُۥ رُوحُ ٱلْقُدُسِ—"The Holy Spirit has sent it down." Ruh al-Qudus is the title given to the angel Jibreel (AS). 

Now, this creates an interesting grammatical point. Typically in the Quran, when we see the verb نَزَّلَ ("He sent down") with the Quran as the object, the subject is Allah ﷻ. We read "We sent down" or "He sent down" referring to Allah ﷻ. But here, the subject of the verb is Jibreel (AS). Does this mean the angel is the one doing the sending independently? 

No—notice the critical qualification: مِن رَّبِّكَ—"from your Lord." The source is clear. Jibreel (AS) is the messenger, but the revelation originates from Allah ﷻ. This is why some scholars have noted that when Allah ﷻ uses "We" in verses about sending down revelation, it may encompass both Himself and the angel who delivers it, though this is a minority opinion. The evidence they cite is precisely this ayah, where the agent is explicitly identified as the angel, but the qualification "from your Lord" maintains the divine source.

Why was it sent? بِٱلْحَقِّ—"with truth." And for what purpose? لِيُثَبِّتَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟—"so that He may strengthen and make firm those who believe." The Quran isn't meant to convince the convinced or prove things to skeptics. Its primary purpose is to give thabat (firmness, stability, strength) to those who already have iman.

Beyond that: وَهُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ—"and as guidance and good news for those who submit." Guidance and glad tidings—these are the fruits of the Quran for those who approach it with submission to Allah ﷻ.

translation: And We certainly know that they say, "It is only a human being who teaches the Prophet." The tongue of the one they refer to is foreign, and this Qur'an is [in] a clear Arabic language
commentary:
**وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُۥ بَشَرٌ ۗ لِّسَانُ ٱلَّذِى يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِىٌّ وَهَـٰذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِىٌّ مُّبِينٌ**

"We certainly know that they say, 'He is only being taught by a human being.' The tongue of the one they allude to is foreign, while this is a clear Arabic tongue."

Allah ﷻ now addresses another accusation: وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُۥ بَشَرٌ—"We already know that they are saying that a human being is teaching him." This is a different kind of allegation from the ones made by the pagan Arabs of Makkah, who called the Quran magic, the ravings of a madman or poetry. 

This particular accusation likely came from the Jewish scholars who encountered the Quran. Their claim was more sophisticated: "Someone is teaching him. He's getting an education. This level of knowledge didn't come from these unsophisticated Arabs—he must have a teacher."

Notice something remarkable: embedded within their accusation is an acknowledgment of the Quran's power and wisdom. By saying "someone must be teaching him," they're admitting that the content is far too sophisticated, too knowledgeable, too wise to have originated locally. They recognize its strength even as they try to explain it away.

But their specific claim was this: لِّسَانُ ٱلَّذِى يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِىٌّ—"The tongue of the one they allude to is foreign." The word يُلْحِدُونَ is fascinating. It comes from the root meaning "to deviate" or "to turn aside." When you shoot an arrow at a target and it deviates slightly off course, that's ilhad. When digging a grave, if it curves instead of remaining rectangular, that's lahd. The word captures the idea of deflection, of bending away from the straight path.

Here, it means they're deflecting the credit, turning it away from the truth toward a false explanation. They're attributing the Quran to أَعْجَمِىٌّ—a non-Arab, a foreigner. The word a'jami is the opposite of 'arabi (Arab). It literally refers to someone who has difficulty speaking clearly, someone who stutters or can't articulate properly—a condescending term the Arabs used for foreigners, as if foreigners couldn't speak "properly."

Their claim: "This couldn't have come from the Arabs. We know the Arabs. They're not this sophisticated. This must come from a foreign, non-Arab source."

Allah's response demolishes this argument: وَهَـٰذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِىٌّ مُّبِينٌ—"while this is a clear, manifest Arabic tongue." You won't find clearer Arabic than this. The Quran became—and remains—the eternal standard of eloquent Arabic speech. How could something that represents the pinnacle of Arabic linguistic achievement come from someone whose language is allegedly foreign and unclear? The accusation collapses under its own absurdity.

Yet within this criticism, there's an element of truth they stumbled upon: the source of this book is not that land. It is coming from outside—not from a foreign human teacher, but from beyond the earthly realm entirely. The knowledge isn't locally sourced; it's divinely revealed. They recognized the foreign origin but attributed it to the wrong source.

translation: Indeed, those who do not believe in the verses of Allah - Allah will not guide them, and for them is a painful punishment
commentary:
**إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ لَا يَهْدِيهِمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ**

"Indeed, those who do not believe in the signs of Allah ﷻ—Allah ﷻ will not guide them, and for them is a painful punishment."

This ayah presents a sobering reality about those who persist in disbelief. إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ—"Those who are bent upon not believing in the ayat of Allah ﷻ." Notice it doesn't say "those who don't know" or "those who haven't been exposed to" the signs. It's specifically about those who refuse to believe despite being confronted with clear evidence.

These are people who have seen the miraculous nature of the Quran, who have heard its message, who have encountered its wisdom—yet they choose rejection. They've made up their minds, and now they go out of their way to construct elaborate conspiracy theories to explain away what's right in front of them. They're willing to believe in far-fetched explanations rather than accept the obvious truth.

For such people: لَا يَهْدِيهِمُ ٱللَّهُ—"Allah ﷻ will not guide them." They've chosen their path, they've closed their hearts to guidance, and so Allah ﷻ doesn't force guidance upon them. They've selected darkness, and they remain in it by their own choice.

وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ—"And for them is painful punishment." The word أَلِيمٌ doesn't just mean painful—it suggests a pain that penetrates, that reaches deep and lingers.

translation: They only invent falsehood who do not believe in the verses of Allah, and it is those who are the liars
commentary:
**إِنَّمَا يَفْتَرِى ٱلْكَذِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰذِبُونَ**

"The ones who fabricate lies are only those who do not believe in the signs of Allah ﷻ, and it is they who are the true liars."

The surah concludes this passage by turning the tables completely. Throughout these ayahs, the disbelievers have been accusing the Prophet ﷺ of fabrication, of making things up, of having a foreign teacher, of being inconsistent. Now Allah ﷻ delivers the final verdict.

إِنَّمَا يَفْتَرِى ٱلْكَذِبَ—"The ones fabricating lies"—who are they? Not the Prophet ﷺ, but ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ—"those who refuse to believe in the signs of Allah ﷻ." They are the fabricators. They're the ones constructing false narratives, inventing baseless accusations, creating conspiracy theories to explain away divine revelation.

وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰذِبُونَ—"And it is they who are the real liars." The word ٱلْكَـٰذِبُونَ is emphatic—*the* liars, the ultimate liars, the ones who truly embody falsehood.

What makes their lie so severe? They know better. Deep down inside, they recognize the truth. Many of them, especially the Jewish scholars mentioned in this context, found confirmation in their own scriptures. They knew this was authentic revelation. Yet they denied it publicly, constructed elaborate justifications for their rejection and spread accusations against the Messenger ﷺ they knew were false.

They are the real liars—not the one who speaks only what revelation brings, but those who deny what their own hearts have recognized as truth.

translation: Whoever disbelieves in Allah after his belief... except for one who is forced [to renounce his religion] while his heart is secure in faith. But those who [willingly] open their breasts to disbelief, upon them is wrath from Allah, and for them is a great punishment
commentary:
مَن كَفَرَ بِٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ إِيمَـٰنِهِۦٓ إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُۥ مُطْمَئِنٌّۢ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ وَلَـٰكِن مَّن شَرَحَ بِٱلْكُفْرِ صَدْرًا فَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ doesn't merely give us commentary on the hearts of those who turn away from faith. He actually opens a window into their hearts, showing us what lies within. The ayah speaks of those who disbelieve in Allah ﷻ after having come to faith. They had already believed, then made the conscious choice to disbelieve.

There is one critical exception: someone who was coerced and forced to deny faith outwardly, while their heart remains completely at rest with Islam. This person knows with certainty that Islam is the truth, but remains silent only because they face life-threatening danger. This exception addresses cases of genuine duress where external pressure conflicts with internal conviction.

But then comes a chilling contrast. Allah ﷻ describes those whose chests became expanded with disbelief. The word شَرَحَ means to expand, which is why we call detailed explanations "sharh" – they expand upon the text. When someone's chest becomes expanded with disbelief, it means they're at ease with it. They've come to love their atheism or their rejection of truth. They say, "Life has no purpose? I'm completely fine with that."

Upon them will be wrath from Allah ﷻ, and they will have a tremendous punishment. When you become satisfied with disbelief, that represents the worst possible state, because it means your conscience has essentially died. If you still have fitrah inside you – that natural goodness – then the filth that has invaded your heart won't let you be at peace. But when you become comfortable with filth, it means your sensitivity to goodness is gone.

Think of walking into a room with a terrible smell. At first you're overwhelmed, but after sitting there for thirty minutes, you don't notice it anymore. You've become accustomed to it. This is what happens when people surround themselves with spiritual filth – they eventually stop noticing it. They become completely happy in that state, which is truly wretched because now they don't even want to change.

translation: That is because they preferred the worldly life over the Hereafter and that Allah does not guide the disbelieving people
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ ٱسْتَحَبُّوا۟ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا عَلَى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

Allah ﷻ explains why this spiritual numbness occurs: it's because they gave their love and preference to worldly life over the Hereafter. When all of your love and attention becomes devoted to worldly life, you lose all sensitivity to the next life. The word اسْتَحَبُّوا indicates they actively chose to love this world more. This wasn't passive neglect – it was an active preference.

Allah ﷻ does not guide the disbelieving nation. When people make this fundamental choice to prioritize the temporary over the eternal, they cut themselves off from divine guidance.

translation: Those are the ones over whose hearts and hearing and vision Allah has sealed, and it is those who are the heedless
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ طَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَسَمْعِهِمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰفِلُونَ

These are the people upon whose hearts, hearing and eyes Allah ﷻ has placed a seal. First, Allah ﷻ described them as satisfied with their disbelief. Now He calls them الْغَـٰفِلُونَ – the utterly heedless. Someone in a state of ghaflah has no idea what's happening around them. They're completely tranquil and happy in their environment, no longer sensitive to anything beyond it.

They've lost awareness that there's such a thing as clean air, that there's a better message, that there's something more worthy of filling their hearts. That sensitivity is completely gone. They're like someone who's been in a contaminated environment so long that they can no longer recognize purity.

translation: Assuredly, it is they, in the Hereafter, who will be the losers
commentary:
لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ

لَا جَرَمَ is an emphatic expression meaning "no doubt about it" or "I swear by Allah." These people, especially in the Hereafter, will be the ultimate losers. They will be the ones who lost everything that truly mattered.

translation: Then, indeed your Lord, to those who emigrated after they had been compelled [to renounce their religion] and thereafter fought [for the cause of Allah] and were patient - indeed, your Lord, after that, is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا فُتِنُوا۟ ثُمَّ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ وَصَبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهَا لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Now Allah ﷻ turns to the other side of the equation. Your Master is certainly forgiving toward those who migrated after they were tested, then struggled and remained patient. This ayah alludes to events yet to come when it was revealed. The Hijrah hasn't happened yet, the major jihad hasn't begun, but Allah ﷻ is foreshadowing what's around the corner. This tells us this is very late Makkan discourse.

The word فُتِنُوا۟ means they were attacked and put to severe tests. These believers will migrate after enduring persecution, then engage in struggle (jihad), and demonstrate sabr – perseverance through hardship. After all they will go through, your Master will be extremely forgiving and perpetually merciful toward them.

translation: On the Day when every soul will come disputing for itself, and every soul will be fully compensated for what it did, and they will not be wronged
commentary:
۞ يَوْمَ تَأْتِى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ تُجَـٰدِلُ عَن نَّفْسِهَا وَتُوَفَّىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ

This forgiveness and mercy will matter most on a Day when every soul will come arguing on behalf of itself. The word تُجَـٰدِلُ means to argue vigorously, to debate fiercely. Everyone will become their own defense attorney, desperately trying to save themselves.

But no matter how much anyone argues, the verdict is already clear: every soul will be given exactly what it earned, precisely what it worked toward. They will not be wronged in the slightest. No one will receive punishment they don't deserve, and no one's good deeds will be diminished.

translation: And Allah presents an example: a city which was safe and secure, its provision coming to it in abundance from every location, but it denied the favors of Allah. So Allah made it taste the envelopment of hunger and fear for what they had been doing
commentary:
وَضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا قَرْيَةً كَانَتْ ءَامِنَةً مُّطْمَئِنَّةً يَأْتِيهَا رِزْقُهَا رَغَدًا مِّن كُلِّ مَكَانٍ فَكَفَرَتْ بِأَنْعُمِ ٱللَّهِ فَأَذَٰقَهَا ٱللَّهُ لِبَاسَ ٱلْجُوعِ وَٱلْخَوْفِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ

Allah ﷻ presents an example of a town that used to be at peace and satisfied. Notice the parallel: earlier we saw a person satisfied in their disbelief, and now Allah ﷻ describes an entire town satisfied with their status quo. They were happy with how things were.

Its provisions came to it freely, without effort, رَغَدًا – abundantly from every direction. Trade flowed in from every coast, making it a central hub. Most scholars agree this describes Makkah, though some suggest it could apply to other places as well. The Quraysh enjoyed exactly this situation.

Then this town disbelieved in Allah's favors. Allah ﷻ made them taste the garment of hunger and fear. This powerful metaphor describes hunger and fear wrapping around the city like clothing wraps a person. Clothing is intimate, inescapable, all-encompassing – that's how these afflictions engulfed them.

Notice the sequence: hunger comes first, then fear. This remains a sociological reality. When the economy collapses and jobs disappear, what rises next? Crime rates. Fear follows hunger. When young people can't find work and economic stability vanishes, social breakdown follows. This is what they manufactured for themselves through their own actions.

Some historians note that after the Prophet ﷺ was rejected, Makkah experienced severe famine and drought. Others suggest this is a warning of what could happen. What's remarkable is that Allah ﷻ doesn't explicitly name the city – He gives enough parallels that anyone in Makkah would think, "That sounds like us," yet the punishment hasn't fully arrived. The message is clear: here's what you should do to prevent it from happening.

This pattern applies to modern societies as well. Nations build economies on forbidden foundations – usury, exploitation, manufactured crises. Eventually comes economic collapse, followed by fear and rising crime. The lesson transcends time and place.

translation: And there had certainly come to them a Messenger from among themselves, but they denied him; so punishment overtook them while they were wrongdoers
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَهُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ

A messenger truly came to them from among themselves. They knew him, he was one of them, he spoke their language and understood their culture. Yet they rejected him and called him a liar. So punishment seized them while they were doing wrong. The consequence came as a direct result of their choice to reject guidance when it came in the clearest, most accessible form.

translation: Then eat of what Allah has provided for you [which is] lawful and good. And be grateful for the favor of Allah, if it is [indeed] Him that you worship
commentary:
فَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ

The instruction comes: learn your lesson from that example. Don't become an example yourselves. Allah ﷻ gave a parable rather than saying directly "you" – leaving enough ambiguity that the Makkans could recognize themselves while still having the opportunity to change course before it's too late.

Consume from what Allah ﷻ has provided you – things that are halal and tayyib. Halal here doesn't primarily mean ritually slaughtered meat, though that will be addressed. The primary meaning concerns wealth earned without cheating, scamming, interest, gambling or other corrupt means. This applies to all societies, not just Muslims.

Be grateful for Allah's favor, if you truly worship Him alone. Gratitude and proper worship are inseparable. You cannot claim to worship Allah ﷻ while being ungrateful for His provisions or while consuming what He has forbidden.

translation: He has only forbidden to you dead animals, blood, the flesh of swine, and that which has been dedicated to other than Allah. But whoever is forced [by necessity], neither desiring [it] nor transgressing [its limit] - then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَيْتَةَ وَٱلدَّمَ وَلَحْمَ ٱلْخِنزِيرِ وَمَآ أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ بِهِۦ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ بَاغٍ وَلَا عَادٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Speaking of halal, let me give you specific guidelines. This reveals something significant: we usually think Islamic law was revealed in Madinah, but some crucial aspects were established even in Makkah. This shows how foundational these matters are.

The prohibitions mentioned here – dead animals, blood, pork – were already referenced in Makkah. Hints about alcohol and gambling also appeared in Makkan revelations, with more detailed rulings coming later in Madinah.

Allah ﷻ has forbidden you dead meat, blood, the flesh of swine, and that over which any name other than Allah's has been invoked. The word أُهِلَّ comes from the root meaning to pronounce something loudly and famously. Originally connected to هِلَال (the crescent moon), because people would see the new moon and loudly proclaim their false gods' names, or announce the moon's appearance at famous gatherings. So when animals were slaughtered with names other than Allah ﷻ being pronounced over them, this fell under the prohibition.

But whoever is compelled by necessity – neither desiring to disobey nor transgressing – then Allah ﷻ is Forgiving and Merciful. The person must not have a rebellious urge against their religion, nor eat it out of animosity. An example of عَادٍ (transgressing) would be like certain groups who specifically refuse to eat halal food, making the only prohibited food for them the food Muslims are allowed. Some hold such hatred that their primary dietary restriction is simply "don't eat what Muslims eat."

translation: And do not say about what your tongues assert of untruth, "This is lawful and this is unlawful," to invent falsehood about Allah. Indeed, those who invent falsehood about Allah will not succeed
commentary:
وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ لِمَا تَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُكُمُ ٱلْكَذِبَ هَـٰذَا حَلَـٰلٌ وَهَـٰذَا حَرَامٌ لِّتَفْتَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ لَا يُفْلِحُونَ

This addresses a sophisticated form of deception. Don't say, based on what your tongues describe – which is nothing but lies – "This is halal and this is haram." The emphasis on تَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُكُمُ (your tongues describe) indicates people don't just make simple declarations. They construct elaborate rationales and present compelling cases. They build detailed arguments, but at the foundation, it's all their own invention about what should be halal and what should be haram.

This happens across the spectrum. Some extremely liberal voices use compelling language: "Islam is a religion of mercy, Islam wouldn't restrict this, Islam's spirit is freedom..." They slip things in that sound convincing but lack textual foundation. On the opposite end, overly strict voices declare, "How can you believe this is acceptable? It obviously contradicts Islamic principles..." and they make passionate arguments without citing a single clear ayah or authentic hadith.

Both extremes share the same problem: eloquent tongues running without knowledge, trying to invent from lies against Allah ﷻ. Those who fabricate lies against Allah ﷻ will never succeed.

translation: [It is but] a brief enjoyment, and they will have a painful punishment
commentary:
مَتَـٰعٌ قَلِيلٌ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

Someone might look around and say, "What do you mean they won't succeed? Look how well they're doing!" Allah ﷻ responds: this is متاع قليل – just a little temporary enjoyment, a brief utility. Don't mistake this for real success. And they have a painful punishment ahead of them.

translation: And to those who are Jews We have prohibited that which We related to you before. And We did not wrong them [thereby], but they were wronging themselves
commentary:
وَعَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ حَرَّمْنَا مَا قَصَصْنَا عَلَيْكَ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَمَا ظَلَمْنَـٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ

Upon those who were Jewish – الَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟, those who turned back to Allah ﷻ, who identified with the tradition of returning to Him – We had forbidden what We already narrated to you before. The dietary restrictions they received were different from yours, but equally from divine revelation.

We never wronged them at all. Rather, they used to wrong themselves. When people claim Allah's laws are burdensome or unfair, the reality is that any difficulty comes from their own choices and rebellion, not from divine injustice.

translation: Then, indeed your Lord, to those who have done wrong out of ignorance and then repent after that and correct themselves - indeed, your Lord, thereafter, is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا۟ ٱلسُّوٓءَ بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ ثُمَّ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوٓا۟ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهَا لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

The surah discussed halal and haram, then mentions people who did wrong while being overwhelmed. جَهَـٰلَة here doesn't mean ignorance in the sense of not knowing better. It means someone who couldn't resist their temptations, who gave in to their desires. This is different from رَفْعٌ (innocent mistake) or عَدَمٌ (simple ignorance).

This describes someone who knew something was prohibited – perhaps they knew they shouldn't consume that food, or engage in that transaction – but were overcome by their nafs, their lower desires. Then they repented after that, and corrected their ways.

Your Master, even after all of that, will be extremely Forgiving, perpetually Merciful. The repetition of this formula throughout these passages emphasizes that no matter how serious the violation, sincere repentance coupled with genuine correction of behavior opens the door to divine forgiveness. Allah's mercy encompasses even those who fell while knowing better, as long as they return to Him with sincerity and make real changes in their lives.

translation: Indeed, Abraham was a [comprehensive] leader, devoutly obedient to Allah, inclining toward truth, and he was not of those who associate others with Allah
commentary:
إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ كَانَ أُمَّةً قَانِتًا لِّلَّهِ حَنِيفًا وَلَمْ يَكُ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ

Allah ﷻ declares that Ibrahim (AS) was an ummah unto himself—a nation all by himself. The word ummah comes from the verb amma, meaning to have a direction or to aim for something. An ummah is a nation unified by a singular direction. Yet here, Allah ﷻ describes Ibrahim (AS) as embodying an entire nation within his own person.

This has profound implications. Ibrahim (AS) spent much of his life as a lone traveler, journeying from land to land with no one beside him. Despite this apparent isolation, Allah ﷻ considers him a complete nation. He carried within himself the potential to father countless nations, and indeed, we still consider ourselves part of the millah—the legacy—of our father Ibrahim (AS).

The description continues: قَانِتًا لِّلَّهِ —completely devoted to Allah. The word qanit is one of the most beautiful terms for obedience in the Arabic language. While words like muti and tabi also mean obedient, qanit carries additional dimensions. It describes an obedience characterized by concentration, joy, subservience and humility. A qanit person not only obeys but finds deep joy in that obedience. They are eager to please, ready to listen to the next instruction without any sense of pride. They are completely humbled before the One they serve.

The ayah adds حَنِيفًا —solely focused on Allah ﷻ without any deviation or bias. The word hanif refers to one who turns away from all falsehood and directs themselves purely toward truth. Then comes the emphatic statement: وَلَمْ يَكُ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ —he was not at all from those who committed shirk. The shortened form "lam yaku" instead of "lam yakun" emphasizes this negation even further. Throughout his entire life, Ibrahim (AS) never fell into association of partners with Allah ﷻ.

translation: [He was] grateful for His favors. Allah chose him and guided him to a straight path
commentary:
شَاكِرًا لِّأَنْعُمِهِ ۚ ٱجْتَبَىٰهُ وَهَدَىٰهُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

Ibrahim (AS) was شَاكِرًا لِّأَنْعُمِهِ —constantly grateful for the favors Allah ﷻ had given him. Notice the word used here: an'umihi, referring to "few favors" rather than ni'amihi which would mean "many blessings." This is significant. Earlier in this surah, the people of Makkah were reminded of how blessings came to them from every direction. This was the manifestation of Ibrahim's (AS) own dua when he said, "Provide them with fruits." Yet Ibrahim (AS) himself, whose supplication brought these luxuries, remained grateful for what he considered few favors from Allah ﷻ.

This teaches us something profound: a lifetime is only enough to thank Allah ﷻ for a few of His blessings. Allah ﷻ is not asking us to thank Him for every single favor because that would be impossible for any creation. We can only do what we can—be grateful for what we can recognize and express thanks for what we're able to comprehend.

The ayah continues: ٱجْتَبَىٰهُ —He chose him for the special qualities he possessed. The verb ijtaba means to select someone because of something distinctive they have. وَهَدَىٰهُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ —and He guided him to a straight path. This straight path led Ibrahim (AS) all the way to Makkah, to the very place where he would leave his family in fulfillment of Allah's command.

translation: And We gave him good in this world, and indeed, in the Hereafter he will be among the righteous
commentary:
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

Allah ﷻ says He gave Ibrahim (AS) حَسَنَةً —goodness in this worldly life. When materialistic people think of "goodness in this life," they imagine wealth, property, comfort and ease. But consider what Ibrahim's (AS) life actually contained: torture, imprisonment, the command to sacrifice his son, separation from his family, one trial after another. Yet because he passed these tests of obedience, this itself was the hasanah—the goodness—Allah ﷻ gave him in this life.

This transforms our understanding of the dua رَبَّنَا آتِنَا فِي الدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً —"Our Lord, give us goodness in this world." The goodness we should seek isn't necessarily material comfort, but the strength and success to pass the tests Allah ﷻ places before us.

The ayah concludes: وَإِنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ —and indeed in the Hereafter he will be among the righteous. This is particularly beautiful because when Allah ﷻ describes the ranks of believers, He mentions the prophets (nabiyyin), the truthful (siddiqin), the martyrs (shuhada) and the righteous (salihin). The righteous—as-salihin—is the broadest category, the one accessible to the most people. 

By describing Ibrahim (AS) as being among the saliheen, Allah ﷻ shows us that even those who just barely make it into Paradise will be able to meet their father Ibrahim (AS). He had all types of spiritual children, and he wants good for all of them. The least qualified of his descendants who enter Paradise—the saliheen—will still have access to him. This reflects his mercy and his desire to be reunited with his entire spiritual family.

translation: Then We revealed to you, [O Muhammad], to follow the religion of Abraham, inclining toward truth; and he was not of those who associate with Allah
commentary:
ثُمَّ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ أَنِ ٱتَّبِعْ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ حَنِيفًا ۖ وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ

Now Allah ﷻ addresses the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ directly: ثُمَّ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ —then We revealed to you—أَنِ ٱتَّبِعْ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ —to follow the way of Ibrahim (AS). The word millah refers to a complete way of life, a legacy, a tradition. The Prophet ﷺ has been given this revelation to complete and fulfill the legacy that Ibrahim (AS) began.

The description repeats: حَنِيفًا —purely dedicated to Allah alone. But notice the shift in how shirk is negated. In ayah 120, it said وَلَمْ يَكُ —"he was not," using the incomplete negative which suggests answering an unspoken question. But here it says وَمَا كَانَ —"he was not at all," using the complete negative form.

Why this difference? In the first instance, Allah ﷻ was correcting those who didn't know Ibrahim's (AS) true nature. But here, He's addressing those who claim Ibrahim (AS) for themselves—particularly the Jews and polytheists of Arabia who both claimed to follow Ibrahim (AS). To them, Allah ﷻ emphatically states: he was absolutely not from among those who committed shirk. This forcefully removes their false claim of connection to him.

translation: The sabbath was only appointed for those who differed over it. And indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ
commentary:
إِنَّمَا جُعِلَ ٱلسَّبْتُ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَيَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ

This ayah continues the conversation with the Jewish community. إِنَّمَا جُعِلَ ٱلسَّبْتُ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ —the Sabbath was only placed upon those who disagreed about it. 

This hints at an important historical reality. Some scholars argue that originally, the Bani Israel weren't given the strict Sabbath restrictions. They were given a more general day of rest and worship, similar to what Muslims have with Jumu'ah. But because they fell into disagreement and dispute about these matters, Allah ﷻ placed the strict Sabbath laws upon them as a test and, in some sense, as a consequence of their disputes.

The ayah concludes: وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَيَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ —and indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that in which they used to differ. All their disputes, arguments and disagreements will be settled by Allah ﷻ Himself on the Day of Standing.

translation: Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good instruction, and argue with them in a way that is best. Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who has strayed from His way, and He is most knowing of who is [rightly] guided
commentary:
ٱدْعُ إِلَىٰ سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ بِٱلْحِكْمَةِ وَٱلْمَوْعِظَةِ ٱلْحَسَنَةِ ۖ وَجَـٰدِلْهُم بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۖ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ

This final ayah of our passage presents a comprehensive methodology for calling people to Islam. Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ—and by extension, all of us: ٱدْعُ إِلَىٰ سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ —call to the path of your Lord.

Three distinct approaches are outlined:

**First: بِٱلْحِكْمَةِ —with wisdom.** This represents the highest level of engagement, meant for the intellectual elite of society. In every community, there exists a group of people who are deeply engaged with ideas, who study intensively, who shape the intellectual atmosphere of their time. In the time of the Quraysh, this included the poets and tribal leaders. In the time of Musa (AS), this included Pharaoh's magicians. These people require sophisticated engagement at their level. They need to see the depth, complexity and intellectual rigor of Islam. They need arguments and evidences that speak to their level of understanding.

**Second: وَٱلْمَوْعِظَةِ ٱلْحَسَنَةِ —with beautiful, good counsel.** This is for the general masses of people. They don't need complex philosophical arguments or deep academic discourse. They need clear, sincere, heartfelt advice. This is what khutbahs, lectures, study circles and informal conversations about faith provide. This is the most accessible level—every Muslim who is awake and aware should be capable of sharing good counsel with others. You don't need to be a scholar to remind a friend not to waste their time, or to encourage family members toward prayer, or to share a meaningful ayah. The Prophet ﷺ said, "Convey from me even if it is a single ayah." This is the maw'idhah al-hasanah—sincere, beautiful counsel that penetrates the heart.

**Third: وَجَـٰدِلْهُم بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ —and debate with them in a way that is better.** This addresses those who actively oppose and attack Islam. Notice it doesn't say "debate with the best argument" but rather "with that which is better." This means when they come at you with a certain level of argument, you respond with something better. When they use foul language, you respond with clean language. When they use poor character, you respond with excellent character. You don't just counter their intellectual arguments—you elevate the entire discourse through superior ethics, mannerisms and conduct. You answer to a higher code.

These three levels address three distinct groups that exist in every society: the intellectual elite who require wisdom, the common people who need good counsel, and the aggressive defenders of the status quo who must be engaged with better arguments and better character.

The ayah concludes with a reminder of ultimate accountability: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۖ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ —indeed your Lord knows best who has gone astray from His path, and He knows best who are the guided ones.

This final statement is liberating. Our job isn't to force guidance into people's hearts—that's beyond our capability. Our responsibility is only to convey the message with wisdom, beautiful counsel and better arguments. The results are in Allah's hands. He alone knows who will ultimately be guided and who will remain astray. This knowledge should free us from the anxiety of results and allow us to focus on the quality and sincerity of our delivery.

translation: And if you punish [an enemy, O believers], punish with an equivalent of that with which you were harmed. But if you are patient - it is better for those who are patient
commentary:
وَإِنْ عَاقَبْتُمْ فَعَاقِبُوا۟ بِمِثْلِ مَا عُوقِبْتُم بِهِۦ ۖ وَلَئِن صَبَرْتُمْ لَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لِّلصَّـٰبِرِينَ

*"And if you punish [an enemy, O believers], punish with an equivalent of that with which you were harmed. But if you are patient—it is better for those who are patient."*

This ayah shifts the focus from the intellectual and spiritual dimensions of da'wah to the physical realities that inevitably follow. When the call to Allah ﷻ begins succeeding at all levels—when wisdom reaches the seekers, when good counsel reaches the masses, and when superior arguments reach the defenders of falsehood—what comes next is predictable: persecution.

### The Pattern of All Prophets

This has been the pattern throughout history. Every messenger who called people to truth faced opposition. They weren't just debated; they were attacked. The messengers engaged with common people, with intellectual elites, and with political powers—covering all three levels of society we discussed in the previous ayah. And once they made that comprehensive engagement, persecution became inevitable.

The word عَاقَبْتُمْ (you punish) comes from the root 'aqaba, which fundamentally means to follow closely, to come after something. The word 'uqubah (punishment) in this context specifically refers to retaliatory punishment—a response to an initial aggression. By using this term, Allah ﷻ is already implying something crucial: the Muslims will be attacked first. The very structure of the ayah assumes that harm will come to them, and it addresses how they should respond when—not if—that happens.

### The Principle of Proportionate Response

The instruction is clear: وَإِنْ عَاقَبْتُمْ فَعَاقِبُوا۟ بِمِثْلِ مَا عُوقِبْتُم بِهِۦ—"if you retaliate, then retaliate with the equivalent of what was done to you." This is the principle of proportionate response, what we might call "an eye for an eye" or "tit for tat" in English. But notice who initiated the aggression in every prophetic narrative: the opponents attacked first, whether ideologically or physically. The Muslims' response would always be reactive, never initiating the conflict.

This ayah was revealed in Makkah, before the Muslims had even considered the possibility of physical retaliation. The Sahabah (RA) hadn't yet migrated to Madinah, where they would eventually be permitted to defend themselves militarily. So why does Allah ﷻ mention the option of retaliation here? Because He's opening a door to the future, preparing them psychologically for what's coming. There will be a time when you can respond, a time when defense becomes necessary and permissible.

### The Superior Path of Patience

But immediately after presenting this option, Allah ﷻ says: وَلَئِن صَبَرْتُمْ لَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لِّلصَّـٰبِرِينَ—"But if you are patient, it is truly better for those who have patience." The structure here is significant. The believers as a group are told "if you are patient, it's better," leaving the option open. But notice what comes in the very next ayah.

translation: And be patient, [O Muhammad], and your patience is not but through Allah. And do not grieve over them and do not be in distress over what they conspire
commentary:
وَٱصْبِرْ وَمَا صَبْرُكَ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ ۚ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا تَكُ فِى ضَيْقٍ مِّمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ

*"And be patient, [O Muhammad], and your patience is not but through Allah. And do not grieve over them or be in distress over what they conspire."*

While the believers were given a conditional statement about patience—"if you are patient"—the Prophet ﷺ receives a direct command: وَٱصْبِرْ—"Be patient." Not "if you are patient," but "be patient." This is his policy, his directive from Allah ﷻ. And if the leader takes this path of patience, then naturally his followers will align with that decision. For now, the community will not retaliate physically. They will absorb the persecution with patience.

### The Source of Patience

But Allah ﷻ immediately clarifies something profound: وَمَا صَبْرُكَ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ—"Your patience is not except through Allah." This isn't a command to muster up willpower through sheer determination. Allah ﷻ is making it clear that the capability to withstand difficulty, the strength to remain patient under persecution, comes from Him alone. The Prophet ﷺ doesn't rely on his own resources, his own community's strength, or political alliances. His sabr (patience) is dependent entirely on Allah ﷻ.

This is a crucial lesson for anyone engaged in calling people to truth. When you face opposition—whether intellectual criticism, social mockery, or even physical harm—your ability to continue comes from Allah ﷻ, not from your own strength.

### Don't Grieve Over Them

Then comes the instruction: وَلَا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ—"And do not grieve over them." Over whom? Over those who reject the message, who persist in their opposition. This addresses a natural human tendency: when you care deeply about sharing truth with people, their rejection can cause real grief. You want them to be guided, you see the harm they're bringing upon themselves, and it hurts when they refuse to listen.

But Allah ﷻ tells the Prophet ﷺ not to carry that grief. Why? Because guidance is in Allah's ﷻ hands, not ours. We're responsible for conveying the message with wisdom, good counsel and strong arguments—as outlined in Ayah 125—but we're not responsible for the outcome. The previous ayah already stated: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ—"Indeed, your Lord knows best who has strayed from His path." He knows, and He'll handle the results.

### Don't Be Distressed by Their Plots

Finally: وَلَا تَكُ فِى ضَيْقٍ مِّمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ—"And do not be in distress over what they conspire." The word ضَيْق means constriction, tightness, discomfort—specifically a tightness in the chest that comes from anxiety or frustration. Allah ﷻ is telling the Prophet ﷺ not to let his chest become constricted because of the schemes and plots being made against him and his message.

This is incredibly relevant today. We live in an age where Muslims are acutely aware of what's being planned against them—conferences are held, legislation is passed, media campaigns are launched, all seemingly designed to undermine Islam. The natural response is anxiety: "They're planning this, they're doing that, we have to do something!" Yes, there may be times for organized responses, for writing, for protests, for producing literature. But we should never become entirely reactionary.

### Stay on Mission

The antidote to this anxiety is found in the previous ayah: ٱدْعُ إِلَىٰ سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ—"Call to the way of your Lord." That's the mission. So when Allah ﷻ says وَٱصْبِرْ (be patient), He means be patient with the mission you've been given. Keep calling people with wisdom. Keep offering good counsel. Keep responding to arguments with better arguments. Let them make their plans—you stay focused on your task.

Sabr doesn't mean passive acceptance or complacent waiting. It means staying on course despite obstacles. It means continuing your work even when opposition arises, trusting that Allah ﷻ is handling the outcomes. The Prophet ﷺ didn't stop calling people to Islam because Quraysh plotted against him. He continued his mission, and that continuation itself was an act of sabr.

Moreover, when it comes to responding to those who argue against Islam—the mujadilun—who provides the counter-arguments in the Quran? Allah ﷻ Himself. Throughout the Quran, we see: "They say this—tell them this. They claim that—respond with this." The responses come directly from Allah ﷻ, which is why they're superior. When we study these passages, we're learning the art of argumentation from the ultimate Teacher. We don't need to invent responses out of anxiety; we need to study the responses He's already provided.

translation: Indeed, Allah is with those who fear Him and those who are doers of good
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّٱلَّذِينَ هُم مُّحْسِنُونَ

*"Indeed, Allah is with those who have taqwa and those who are doers of good."*

After giving this comprehensive mission—calling people to Allah's ﷻ path at multiple levels, enduring persecution with patience, not becoming distressed over plots—Allah ﷻ concludes with the most important promise: He is with those who fulfill two conditions.

### The Meaning of "Allah is With"

In Arabic, being "with" someone can mean physical proximity, but it more often signifies support, alliance and backing. When someone says "I'm with you" regarding a difficult task, they mean "I support you in this, I'll stand by you, you have my help." When Allah ﷻ says He is "with" certain people, He's promising them His divine support, His aid, His reinforcement in their mission.

This is monumental. Regardless of whatever plots are being made, regardless of the opposition's resources or strategies, regardless of how impossible the task seems—when Allah ﷻ promises His ma'iyyah (being-with), we have everything we need. No scheme can succeed against those whom Allah ﷻ supports.

### The Two Qualities: Taqwa and Ihsan

But this support isn't unconditional. It's specifically for ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟ (those who have taqwa) وَّٱلَّذِينَ هُم مُّحْسِنُونَ (and those who are doers of ihsan).

Notice the connection to one of the central themes of this entire Surah. Earlier in Surah An-Nahl, Allah ﷻ declared: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْعَدْلِ وَٱلْإِحْسَـٰنِ—"Indeed Allah commands justice and ihsan" (16:90). That ayah presented a progression: 'adl (justice) is the baseline expectation, and ihsan is the elevated goal. Here at the conclusion, we see the same progression between taqwa and ihsan.

**Taqwa** is the essential starting point. It means to protect yourself from Allah's ﷻ displeasure, to guard against actions and attitudes that would anger Him. This is where our relationship with Allah ﷻ must begin—with a healthy consciousness of Him, an awareness that moves us to avoid what displeases Him.

Some people question why Islamic teachings emphasize fear and protection so much. "Is your God a God of fear? What about love?" But understand that taqwa is the foundation that makes love possible. When your heart is protected from sin, when you're not constantly polluting your inner state with disobedience, then your heart has the capacity to grow in its love and appreciation of Allah ﷻ. You cannot build the higher stages of love, hope and excellence on a foundation of ongoing sin. Taqwa clears the path; it removes the barriers. It's not that our entire relationship with Allah ﷻ is based on fear—rather, we begin with a healthy fear that protects us, and from that protected space, our relationship deepens and evolves.

**Ihsan** is the goal, the pinnacle. It means excellence, beauty in action, going beyond what's merely required to what's beautiful. The famous hadith defines it: "That you worship Allah as if you see Him, and if you don't see Him, know that He sees you." This is the ultimate state—when your consciousness of Allah ﷻ becomes so real that it transforms every action into an act of devotion and excellence.

The progression from taqwa to ihsan mirrors the progression from 'adl to ihsan mentioned earlier in the Surah. We start by protecting ourselves (taqwa), fulfilling basic obligations (adl), and we aim to reach a state where we're constantly excelling in goodness (ihsan). This isn't just about individual spirituality—it applies to our entire mission of calling people to Allah's ﷻ path. We engage with wisdom and good counsel (which requires ihsan), we remain patient under persecution (which requires taqwa), and we trust that Allah ﷻ will support this effort.

### The Promise That Sustains

So after commanding the Prophet ﷺ to remain patient, not to grieve over those who reject, not to be anxious about their schemes—after all these difficult instructions—Allah ﷻ provides the ultimate reassurance: I am with you. You have My support. You don't need political power, you don't need military might, you don't need the approval of the elite. What you need is taqwa and ihsan, and when you have those, you have Me.

This is the promise that sustained the Prophet ﷺ through the most difficult years in Makkah. This is the promise that should sustain anyone engaged in calling to Allah's ﷻ path today. When you feel overwhelmed by the opposition, when the plots seem too sophisticated and well-resourced, when your own efforts seem insignificant—remember that Allah ﷻ is with those who have taqwa and do ihsan.

### Staying on Course

The beauty of how this Surah concludes is that it brings us full circle. We were given a mission—comprehensive da'wah at three levels. We were told to expect opposition and given permission to defend ourselves proportionately, but encouraged toward patience. We were commanded not to let anxiety over people's schemes distract us from our mission. And finally, we were promised that if we maintain taqwa and strive for ihsan, Allah ﷻ Himself is with us.

This means we can stay on course. We don't need to constantly react to every attack, every plot, every scheme against Islam or Muslims. We have a mission, and that mission is clear: call to Allah's ﷻ way with wisdom, with good counsel, and with arguments that are better. Do this with taqwa—protecting yourself from what displeases Him—and strive for ihsan, excellence in how you convey this message.

When you do that, you have Allah's ﷻ support. And with His support, no plot can ultimately succeed against you. This doesn't mean there won't be difficulties, persecution or temporary setbacks—the Prophet ﷺ himself faced all of these. But it means that the mission will succeed, the truth will prevail, and those who remained patient and excellent in their work will be vindicated.

The Surah began by reminding us of Allah's ﷻ countless blessings, moved through guidelines on how to live with gratitude and consciousness, addressed how societies rise and fall, established the principles of da'wah, and concludes with this profound promise. As we close our study of these ayahs, we should reflect: Where do I fit in this mission? How can I contribute to calling people to Allah's ﷻ path—whether through wisdom, through good counsel, through better arguments, or through the crucial behind-the-scenes work that makes all of this possible? And most importantly, how can I ensure that I'm among those who have taqwa and strive for ihsan, so that I can have the company and support of Allah ﷻ in whatever role He's chosen for me?
